metadata.source: Seattle Public Utilities — Where Does It Go?

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Contact the supplier or manufacturer. In most cases, they will take the tank back at no cost. If the supplier refuses to accept the tank, call (206) 296-4692. You will need to request approval to bring the tank to a household hazardous waste facility .

items.explanation: Drop empty ACETYLENE OXYGEN TANKS off at the supplier or manufacturer.  They may take the tank back for free. If they refuse to take the tank, call Household Hazardous waste at (206) 296-4692 to request approval to drop it off at their facilities.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Ceiling tiles that don't contain asbestos can go in the garbage: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: If you believe the ceiling tile contains asbestos, contact the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency at (206) 343-8800.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring acoustic ceiling tiles that don't contain asbestos to city transfer stations . Fees apply.

items.explanation: ACOUSTIC CEILING TILE that does not contain asbestos can go in the garbage or it can be dropped off at city transfer stations.  If you think the ceiling tile has asbestos, call the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency at (206) 343-8800.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Empty aerosol cans that had non-hazardous materials in them can be put in the recycling ONLY if the can is completely emptied of contents and pressure.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Empty hazardous material aerosol cans. • Partially full cans with non-hazardous content.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring cans that contain hazardous materials like paint, solvents, bear spray, or other flammables to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. Hazardous products are usually labeled with the words Danger, Poison, Caution, or Warning.

items.explanation: AEROSOL CANS may go in the garbage but only if they are empty or contain non-hazardous material.  Take cans with hazardous materials like paint, solvents, or other flammables to a household hazardous waste facility for free. For facility hours and location go to hazwastehelp.org.

items.item_name: Air Conditioners, Heat Pumps, or Dehumidifiers

disposal_conditions.garbage: Air purifier filters and furnace filters should be individually bagged and put in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring damaged air conditioners, heat pumps, and dehumidifiers to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page .
disposal_conditions.special: Call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to request special item pickup . Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate air conditioners, heat pumps, or dehumidifiers in good condition to local thrift stores . Call first to determine acceptance policies. Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: You can donate AIR CONDITIONERS OR HEAT PUMPS, if they are in good working condition. Call local thrift stores to confirm they will take them, or resell them on sites like Craigslist.  If broken, you can take them to City transfer stations or call to have it picked up for a fee.  To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Batteries can't go in the garbage because they contain materials harmful to people, animals, and the environment.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Bring batteries to a household hazardous waste facility or city transfer station , where the materials will be reclaimed and recycled. No fee. • Accepted batteries include alkaline, button, rechargeable, and lead-acid batteries. • Separate batteries from devices. • Tape the ends of each individual household battery • Damaged and/or recalled batteries are NOT accepted at transfer stations. You can take them to household hazardous waste facility for free. • Call2Recycle is a free take-back program for rechargeable batteries. You can find free drop off locations or shop for mail-back packaging options. They accept: • Rechargeable batteries • Cellphones and cellphone batteries • E-Bike batteries (participating brands) • 1-800-RECYCLE is both a hotline and an online tool that connects you or your business to recycling services across Washington. This tool can help you find where in your community you can recycle everything from used appliances to leftover paint. Fees may apply.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup from your residence. The materials collected will be reclaimed and recycled. Fees apply.

items.explanation: Do not put batteries in your curbside trash or recycling bins. This includes Alkaline, Lithium, Lithium-Ion, Rechargeable, Computer, and Button batteries as well as products that contain batteries that cannot be easily removed. It is important to keep batteries out of your bins because they can cause fires in trucks and transfer stations. Batteries can be correctly disposed of in multiple ways. Batteries can be dropped off at a city transfer station or Hazardous Household Waste facility for free. You can also submit a special item pickup online or by calling 2066843000 to be picked up at your home. Fees apply for this service. You may also call 1800Recycle or go to call2recycle.com for additional drop off locations.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Metal parts larger than 3 inches go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items with plastic, wood, or rubber attached • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • All metal, except car parts, are accepted as recycling at city transfer stations . There is no charge for recycling at the transfer station. • Maximum size is 8 feet long. • There is a charge for appliances, limit 2 per load.
disposal_conditions.special: • Large metal parts can be put at the curb for pickup on your recycling collection day. • These must be bundled or tied. • The maximum size is 2 feet on all sides.

items.explanation: ALUMINUM can be recycled.  It can go in your home recycling if it contains only aluminum and is smaller than 2 feet by 2 feet by 2 feet but  if it's  larger than you can drop off at City transfer stations for free.  Items that have other materials attached or are smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage. For transfer station hours go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Clean and dry aluminum foil should be balled up and larger than 3 inches • Aluminum food pans • Foil and trays must be empty, clean, and dry
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Dirty or food-covered aluminum foil or food pans • Wine bottle foil

items.explanation: ALUMINUM FOIL can be recycled. It needs to be clean and larger than 3 inches.  Dirty foil and little pieces go in the garbage but don't ball it up.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Call the Seattle Police Firing Range at (206) 684-7470 to dispose of ammunition or firearms.

items.explanation: AMMUNITION OR GUNS are hazardous materials. Call the Seattle Police Firing Range at (206) 684-7470 to dispose of ammunition or firearms.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Antifreeze is harmful to people, animals, and the environment and should never be poured down drains, toilets, or on the ground. Bring antifreeze to a household hazardous waste facility : • All containers must be 5-gallons or less. • Limit 50-gallons per trip.

items.explanation: ANTIFREEZE is a  hazardous material. Bring antifreeze to a household hazardous waste facility. All containers must be 5-gallons or less and the limit is 50-gallons per trip.   Antifreeze is harmful to people, animals, and the environment and should never be poured down drains, toilets, or on the ground. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Asbestos is considered a hazardous material that does not belong in the garbage. Because asbestos is harmful to people, animals, and the environment, it must be disposed of at designated facilities. Please bring any asbestos to an asbestos disposal facility near you.

items.explanation: ASBESTOS is a health hazard. Bring to an asbestos disposal facility.  Asbestos is harmful to people, animals, and the environment, it must be disposed of at designated facilities.  Call Puget Sound Clean Air Agency for more information at (206) 343-8800

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Empty milk, juice, and soup cartons • Cartons labeled Tetra Pak • Other aseptic cartons Plastic spouts don't need to be removed to recycle Tetra Pak cartons. Caps should be removed and placed in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Plastic and foil juice and squeeze boxes go in the garbage. • Put plastic straws and caps in the garbage.

items.explanation: ASEPTIC OR TETRA PAK can be recycled. Cartons must be clean and empty. Plastic spouts don't need to be removed. Plastic caps go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Asphalt roofing that doesn't contain asbestos can go in the garbage: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits p for your Garbage cart.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: If you believe the roofing contains asbestos, contact the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency at (206) 343-8800.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring asphalt roofing shingles that don't contain asbestos to city transfer stations . Fees apply.

items.explanation: ASPHALT ROOFING that does not contain asbestos can go in the garbage or It can be dropped off at the transfer station for a fee.  Don't exceed the weight limits for your garbage cart.    If you think the roofing contains asbestos, contact the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency at (206) 343-8800.  For transfer station hours go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Broken auto and window glass can go in the garbage: • Glass must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits for your cart. • Auto and Window glass are a different type of glass than bottles and jars, and cannot be recycled
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring auto glass to city transfer stations . Fees apply.

items.explanation: AUTO GLASS goes in the garbage or it can be dropped off at the transfer station for a fee. Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your bin. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Baking trays and cookie sheets less than two feet in any direction and free of food residue go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate baking trays or cookie sheets in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give them away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: BAKING TRAYS OR COOKIE SHEETS can be donated or they can be recycled in your cart is clean.  You can donate items in good condition to local thrift stores. Resell or give them away using sites like Craigslist, Freecycle, and Buy Nothing.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic and dirty metal drums that held non-hazardous material can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Sealed drums and drums that held hazardous waste must go to King County Household Hazardous Waste .
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring barrels and drums that held non-hazardous material to city transfer stations : • Empty barrels and drums must be clean and cut in half. • Clean metal can be recycled for no fee.

items.explanation: BARRELS OR DRUMS go in the garbage. Or they can be dropped off at Seattle Transfer stations if the drums held non-hazardous material. Drums that held hazardous materials must go to Household Hazardous waste facilities.   Empty barrels and drums must be clean and cut in half. Clean metal can be recycled for free. For transfer station hours go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Metal bed frames can go in recycling. • Rusted metal can be recycled unless the metal is falling apart. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached. • Be careful not to exceed the weight limits on your cart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items with large amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached. • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring bed frames to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page .
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup. Fee applies.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate bed frames in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: BED FRAMES can be donated or resold if in good working condition.  Contact thrift stores to see if they are accepted.  Broken or large frames can be taken to Seattle transfer stations or picked up for a fee.  To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems.  For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Bedding, linens, towels, down feathers, foam pads, and other fabrics can go in the garbage. Non-decorative pillows cannot be donated for Threadcycle and go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate things in good condition to thrift stores or organizations who accept textiles. Even stained, worn out, ripped, or damaged bedding, linens, towels, decorative pillows and other fabrics can be donated to thrift stores and turned into new products through Threadcycle . Some local thrift and second-hand stores are partners and will accept these items at their regular collection or drop box locations.

items.explanation: BEDDING OR LINENS can go in the garbage. Or you can donate items in good condition to thrift stores or resell them.  Ripped and stained fabrics can be donated for textile recycling at thrift stores that are part of the Threadcycle program.  For a list of stores go to kingcounty.gov/threadcycle

disposal_conditions.recycling: Beverage cans: • Empty soda and beverage cans go in the recycling. • Crushed aluminum cans are ok to recycle. Food cans: • Soup and other food cans are recyclable when they are empty, clean and dry. • Keep can lids partially attached or push the lid into the can. You don't need to remove the can label. Pet food cans: • Pet food containers made out of alumunim that are empty, clean, and dry can be recycled, even if they are small.
disposal_conditions.garbage: If an aluminum can has a removable plastic lid, the lid can go in the garbage, but the can should go in the recycling.

items.explanation: BEVERAGE CANS go in the recycling.   It  takes 98% less energy to use recycled aluminum than using new material.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean metal bike parts 3 inches or larger can go in the recycling: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits for your cart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Worn tires, broken inner tubes, and Bike parts smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Drop off small metal parts and loose bike chains in scrap metal bins for recycling. • Bring bikes and parts to city transfer stations to be recycled. No fee.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate or resell usable bikes and parts to scrap metal dealers or community-oriented bike shops like Bike Works or Recycled Cycles .

items.explanation: BICYCLES OR BIKE PARTS can be recycled or dropped off at the Transfer stations for free.  You can donate bikes to Bike Works or Recycled Cycles. Clean metal bike parts larger than 3-inches can go in the recycling.  Small or dirty parts go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Put all blister packages in the garbage. They aren't recyclable. Blister packages are the clear hard-to-open plastic packaging from items such as batteries, toys, and small electronics.

items.explanation: BLISTER PACKAGES go in the garbage.  They are not recyclable. Blister packages are the clear hard to open plastic packaging from items like batteries and small electronics.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Small plastic bottle caps if screwed onto an empty plastic bottle can be put into the recycling • Loose plastic and metal lids 3 inches in diameter or larger
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Loose lids of any material less than 3 inches in diameter • Loose plastic bottle caps less than 3 inches in diameter • Metal beer and soda caps less than 3 inches in diameter
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Put small metal caps, such as beer and soda caps, in a metal can or container so they are not loose and drop off in scrap metal bin at the Transfer Stations.

items.explanation: BOTTLE CAPS OR JAR LIDS can be recycled if you put them back on the container but loose caps go in the garbage if they are smaller than 3 inches.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring fluids to a household hazardous waste facility . Fees may apply. • Don't mix fluids together! • Put fluids in a sealed container that is 5 gallons or less. • Limit 50 gallons per trip

items.explanation: BRAKE OR TRANSMISSION FLUID is a health hazard. Bring this to Household Hazardous waste for free disposal.  For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org.  Don't pour brake and transmission fluids down drains or toilets, down storm drains, or into soil. These all cause water pollution.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic bread bags go in the garbage.

items.explanation: BREAD BAGS go in the garbage because they often contain food residue and moisture that can contaminates recycling.  Also, The City of Seattle no longer accepts plastic bags for recycling.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Brick can go in the garbage if it doesn't exceed the weight limits for your cart size.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Transfer station fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: You can donate bricks in good condition to many building supply stores which specialize in salvaged materials.

items.explanation: BRICKS can go in the garbage. Or you can take them to the transfer station for a fee. You can donate bricks in good condition to many building supply stores which specialize in salvaged materials. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Pieces of broken bottles and jars 3 inches or larger
disposal_conditions.garbage: • All pieces of broken drinking glasses or wine glasses • Pieces of broken bottles and jars smaller than 3 inches • All broken kitchenware glass or baking glass such as Pyrex • All broken frosted glass • Broken aquarium glass

items.explanation: Broken bottles and jars can be recycled. Broken drinking glasses, window, other types of glass or pieces smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean buckets and carts go in your curbside recycling cart or can be set at the curb next to your cart.

items.explanation: BUCKETS go in recyle if they are clean.  Metal handles are okay to leave attached.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean butter boxes and other clean paper food boxes go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: All butter wrappers go in the garbage.

items.explanation: BUTTER BOXES can be recycled if they are clean however butter wrappers go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Soup and other food cans when they are empty, clean and dry. Keep can lids partially attached or push the lid into the can. You don't need to remove the can label. Recycle when the can and lid are clean, empty and dry. Alternatively, the loose can lid can be put into the can and the top crimped to keep the lid inside.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Loose can lids (not attached) go in the garbage. Rusty cans go in the garbage. Cans that are not empty, clean and dry go in the garbage.

items.explanation: CAN LIDS go in the recycling if they are partially attached or pushed into the can.  Loose lids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Metal candle containers can be recycled if the container tin is empty, clean, and dry, and the containers are three inches or greater in diameter. Candle wax and candle glass are not recyclable.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Candle wax and candle glass go in the garbage. Candle wax and candle glass are not recyclable. Some candle glass is made from heat-treated glass. This is a different type of glass than bottle or jar glass that is recyclable. Mixing heat-treated glass with bottle or jar glass during the recycling process can cause the entire collection to be contaminated and no longer recyclable. Tealights, incense, oil wicks, and other accessories go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: You can melt leftover wax by placing the candle glass in hot water. Then pour the candle wax into another candle. You may need a new wick to light the new candle. You can also repurpose candle glass or metal tins for different uses such as drinking cups or storage devices.

items.explanation: CANDLES goes in the garbage. Let candle wax cool before putting it in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: • Batteries can't go in the garbage because they contain materials harmful to people, animals, and the environment.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Household hazardous waste facility Bring batteries to a city household hazardous waste facility , where the materials will be reclaimed and recycled. No fee. Accepted batteries: lead-acid and car batteries. • Separate batteries from devices • Limit 5 lead-acid auto or maine batteries per visit (batteries from electric vehicles are not accepted) • 1-800-RECYCLE is both a hotline and an online tool that connects you or your business to recycling services across Washington. This tool can help you find where in your community you can recycle everything from used appliances to leftover paint. Fees may apply. • Bring vehicle batteries to a city transfer station where the materials will be reclaimed and recycled. No fee. • Limit of 5 car batteries per visit.

items.explanation: CAR BATTERIES can be dropped off for recycling at city transfer stations for a fee, but you can also bring up to 5 vehicle batteries per trip to a household hazardous waste facility for free.  For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations. For household hazardous waste information call the Household Hazards Line at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Plastic lids 3 inches in diameter or larger
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Nut, chip, frozen juice and coffee canisters • Plastic lids less than 3 inches in diameter

items.explanation: CARDBOARD AND METAL CONTAINERS go in the garbage. Cardboard and metal containers like food chip tubes and coffee containers are considered a mixed material that cannot be recycled.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Cardboard and flattened cardboard boxes go in the recycling. You can leave labels and tape on the boxes. Remove any packing materials inside and dispose of properly. • Pet scratch pads made of cardboard are recyclable.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Cardboard with grease or oil stains (like pizza boxes) go in the food and yard waste cart. • Put cardboard that is mostly wet in the food and yard waste cart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Waxed cardboard like produce boxes goes in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.special: Cardboard that doesn't fit can go next to your cart on your recycling day. Bundle and set next to the cart, or put in a box or container next to your cart. Remove foam and packing materials. There is no charge for extra recycling.

items.explanation: CARDBOARD BOXES go in the recycling.  Flatten them before putting in recycling.  Greasy boxes, like pizza boxes, and wet cardboard go in the food and yard waste cart.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Fees apply. Carpet and padding can go in the garbage. Cut into smaller pieces if carpet or padding doesn't fit in your cart with the lid closed. Coir mats go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring carpet and padding up to 8 feet long and 200 pounds to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.special: Call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to request special item pickup . Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate area rugs in good condition to thrift or through reuse community groups. Contact thrifts stores to confirm they will accept area rugs.

items.explanation: CARPET OR PADDING goes in the garbage. Large amounts can be taken to the transfer station or set out for extra garbage collection for a fee.  For amounts larger than a 32 gallon bag,  schedule a pick up at seattle.gov/SpecialItems

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Rusted metal can be recycled unless the metal is falling apart. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items with large amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches
disposal_conditions.hazardous: All metal, except car parts, is accepted as recycling at city transfer stations . There is no charge for recycling at the transfer station. • Maximum size is 8 feet long. • There is a charge for appliances, limit 2 per load.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item for large pieces pickup. Fee applies. Large metal parts can be put at the curb for pickup on your recycling collection day. • These must be bundled or tied. • The maximum size is 2 feet on all sides.

items.explanation: CAST IRON goes in the recycling.  Small amounts of attached plastic are acceptable, such as a pan handle.  Large pieces can be taken to the transfer station for free. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: CD and DVD cases go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Stores that sell used CDs or DVDs may take cases in good condition.

items.explanation: CD or DVD cases go in the garbage. The plastic that CD and DVD cases are made of is not recyclable.

items.item_name: CDs, DVDs, Records, Video Tapes, and Floppy Disks
synonyms: CDs, DVDs, Records, Video Tapes, and Floppy Disks

disposal_conditions.recycling: The cardboard sleeve of vinyl records is recyclable.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • CDs • DVDs • Floppy discs • CD and DVD cases • Video tapes • Records • Vinyl records
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate CDs, DVDs, records, and video tapes in good condition to local thrift stores . • Resell or give away usable CDs and DVDs through sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing . You may also be able to sell records or CDs to your local record store!

items.explanation: You can donate CDS, DVDS, OR FLOPPIES in good condition to local thrift stores or give them away through sites like Craigslist, Freecycle, and Buy Nothing, or you can put them in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean and Empty cereal and cracker boxes go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic liners and trays from food boxes go in the garbage.

items.explanation: CEREAL OR CRACKER BOXES go in the recycling if they are empty.  Plastic liners and trays go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Old and expired child car seats go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate car seats in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: You can donate CHILD CAR SEATS that have not expired and have not been in an accident.  Damaged and expired seats go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Chip bags go in the garbage because they are made from several different materials that can't be separated for recycling.

items.explanation: CHIP BAGS go in the garbage because they are made from several different materials that cannot be separated for recycling.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Office paper, printer paper, and plain paper folders go in the recycling. No need to remove staples. Remove large metal pieces before recycling, such as on hanging file folders.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Paper scraps and cards smaller than 3 inches • Wet or soiled paper • Sandpaper, used or unused • Paper with paint, glitter, decorations, glue, etc. goes in the garbage • Loose staples are garbage, they are too small to be recycled • Laminated paper, and other paper items made of two or more materials that cannot be separated

items.explanation: OFFICE PAPER goes in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.compost: Place up to two completely undecorated and unflocked trees and/or holiday greens without decorations or soil, next to your cart on collection day for free from December 26 through January 31. Trees must be cut to 4-foot lengths and the trunk diameter should not exceed 4 inches. Starting February 1, extra yard waste fee applies
disposal_conditions.dropoff: From December 26 to January 31, bring Christmas trees to a city transfer station for free. Starting February 1, regular fees apply. • Remove all decorations, tree flocking, and wiring • Trees cannot exceed 8 feet in length • Trunks cannot exceed 4 inches in diameter • Stations will accept up to 3 trees per vehicle
disposal_conditions.special: From December 26 to January 31, holiday trees, wreaths, and greens without decorations can be composted for free. Place on the curb next to your food and yard waste cart on your regular collection day. • Flocked and plastic trees or wreaths will be charged as extra garbage • Remove all decorations: lights, tinsel, metal clips, ornaments, and bows • Trees must be cut into lengths to 4-feet or shorter • For bundling branches, use sisal string or twine (not plastic) At apartments, two trees may be placed next to each food and yard waste cart at no extra charge each collection day. Each additional tree will be picked up as extra yard waste ( fees apply ).

items.explanation: CHRISTMAS TREES go in the yard waste. Real trees can be put next to Food and Yard waste carts and picked up for free from after Christmas until the end of January.  All decorations and flocking must be removed. Artificial trees go in the garbage and if they do not fit into the cart, there is an extra garbage fee.

disposal_conditions.compost: Clean, untreated and unpainted wood goes in the food and yard waste cart. • All pieces must be 4 inches or less in diameter and 4 feet or less in length. • Put sawdust or shavings in paper bags or boxes to keep from scattering. • Avoid extra charges -- Items should fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Check the King County What Do I Do With tool to find locations that will take clean wood for repurposing or recycling. Bring untreated wood waste to city transfer stations . • All pieces must be 4 feet or less in length and 4 inches or less in diameter. • Nails and staples are okay.
disposal_conditions.special: Bundles can be set next to the cart. Fees apply. Check rates here . • Tie bundles with natural twine. No plastic allowed. • Bundles must be 2 feet or less in diameter.
disposal_conditions.donate: Many construction materials are salvageable. Check the Salvage Building Materials page to find more information about what materials can be saved.

items.explanation: CLEAN WOOD WASTE can go in the Food and Yard waste, but it must be less than 4 inches in diameter and less than 4 feet in length. Nails and staples are okay. Larger pieces can be picked up as extra yard waste for a fee.  Painted and treated wood goes in the garbage.  You can also drop off at the Transfer station for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Empty non-toxic cleaning supply containers go in the recycling. Empty and rinsed bleach, drain cleaner, and other cleaners bottles go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Non toxic chemicals, such as soaps and detergents can go in the garbage. • All cleaning wipes with or without cleaner, as well as paper towels used to wipe cleaning products, go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring partially used cleaning supplies to a household hazardous waste facility for free drop off.

items.explanation: Drop off CLEANING SUPPLIES at Household Hazardous waste facilities for free. All containers must be 5-gallons or less. Limit 50-gallons per trip. For household hazardous waste information call the Household Hazards Line at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: Clothes and shoes can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Stained, worn out, ripped, or damaged clothing and shoes can be turned into new products through Threadcycle . Some local thrift and second-hand stores are partners and will accept these items at their regular collection or drop box locations.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate gently used clothing to a local thrift store .

items.explanation: CLOTHES OR SHOES can be dropped off for recycling at thrift stores in the Threadcycle program.  Find locations at Kingcounty.gov/threadcycle.  You can also put CLOTHES OR SHOES in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty CO2 chargers under 2 pounds go in the garbage.

items.explanation: CO2 CHARGERS go in the garbage if they are under 2-pounds.  Bring CO2 chargers that are full or partially full to a household hazardous waste facility for free.   For household hazardous waste information call the Household Hazards Line at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.compost: Paper coffee filters are compostable and go in the food and yard waste together with coffee grounds.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic, plastic-coated, or cloth coffee filters go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Paper COFFEE FILTERS go in the yard waste.  Plastic, cloth or plastic coated filters go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Clean, Empty and Dry coffee pods larger than 3 inches in diameter.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Coffee pods are compostable if they are labelled compostable and there is an accompanying certification from BPI, CMA, or TUV. • Aluminum Nespresso pods can be recycled at Nespresso stores or mailed back to Nespresso. For details, see the Nespresso website .
disposal_conditions.garbage: Coffee pods smaller than 3 inches. Wrappers should go in the garbage.

items.explanation: COFFEE PODS go in the garbage.  Some approved compostable pods are accepted in the yard waste.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Wood paneling and composite materials can go in the garbage: • Break paneling down so it fits in your cart with the lid closed. • Make sure it doesn't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring wood paneling and composite materials to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check rates here.
disposal_conditions.special: Schedule a Special Item Pickup online or call (206)684-3000. Fees apply. Paneling that does not fit in your cart with the lid closed can be put in a plastic bag and set next to your garbage cart as EXTRA garbage on collection day. Fees apply. Check rates here.
disposal_conditions.donate: Many household materials in good condition are salvageable. Check the Salvage Building Materials page and the Northwest Building Salvage Network to find more information about what materials can be salvaged and reused.

items.explanation: COMPOSITE WOOD PANELING goes in the garbage.  If it does not fit in your cart, drop off at the transfer station or set it out as extra garbage for a fee.  Maximum extra garbage set out is 60 lbs. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.compost: Bags labeled "Compostable" from approved brands go in the food and yard waste. Approved compostable plastic bags are green or brown tinted and are clearly labeled ‘Compostable’.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Bags that aren't from approved brands or do not say 'Compostable' on them go in the garbage. Biodegradable bags are not compostable, and go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Approved COMPOSTABLE BAGS go in the yard waste.  Bags labled "biodegradable" are actually not ok for yard waste and must go in the garbage.  More information about approved bags can be found by searching the seattle.gov where does it go website.  Go to this address: www.seattle.gov/utilities/services/food-and-yard/food-and-yard-waste-at-home/approved-compostable-bags

disposal_conditions.compost: • Items labeled “Compostable,” and have a certification mark such as BPI, TÜV, or CMA, go in the food and yard waste. • Compostable food service packaging items may include: • Clamshell-style, hinge-lid paper fiber To-Go food containers • Fold-top To-Go paper boxes • Cups • Plates • Straws and utensils • Portion cups • Paper napkins • Paper towels • Uncoated (not shiny) paper plates and bowls (such as Chinet brand) • Sugar packets Learn more about compostable packaging (PDF).
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic stickers or labels must be removed from compostable food packaging before going in the compost cart. How to identify plastic stickers and labels: • harder to tear • elastic or stretch when attempting to remove • shiny • cannot see fibers when cut

items.explanation: COMPOSTABLE FOOD SERVICE ITEMS go in the yard waste.  Items labeled Compostable, #7 PLA and paper items without a shiny coating are compostable. All other items if dirty go in the garbage. This includes utensils, plates, cups, and containers.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Concrete and asphalt can go in the garbage if it doesn't exceed the weight limits , and fits in your cart with the lid closed. Each concrete or asphalt piece must be less than 2 cubic feet.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Check the King County What Do I Do With tool to find locations that will take concrete or asphalt for recycling. Bring concrete and asphalt to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check rates here. • Each concrete or asphalt piece must be less than 2' x 2' x 2' and less than 200 lbs.
disposal_conditions.donate: Many construction materials are salvageable. Check the Salvage Building Materials page to find more information about what materials can be saved.

items.explanation: Concrete and asphalt can go in the garbage if it doesn't exceed the weight limits, and fits in your bin with the lid closed. Each concrete or asphalt piece must be less than 2 cubic feet.   You can bring concrete and asphalt to city transfer stations.  Each piece must be less than 2' x 2' x 2' and less than 200 lbs.  For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Metal food containers like oil jugs or cookie tins are recyclable if they are empty, clean, and dry.

items.explanation: COOKIE TINS go in the recycling. They should be clean and empty.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Metal parts larger than 3 inches go in the recycling. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items with large amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches
disposal_conditions.hazardous: All metal, except car parts, is accepted as recycling at city transfer stations . There is no fee for most metal. • Maximum size is 8 feet long. • There is a charge for appliances, limit 2 per load.
disposal_conditions.special: For large items: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to request bulky item collection. Fees apply. Large metal parts can be put at the curb for pickup on your recycling collection day. • These must be bundled or tied. • The maximum size is 2 feet on all sides.

items.explanation: COPPER, BRONZE OR BRASS goes in the recycling.  Or it can be dropped off at the Transfer station for free. If it has small amounts of other non-metal material, it is acceptable.  Pieces should be larger than 3 inches and smaller than 2 feet by 2 feet.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Corks, both natural and synthetic (including those made out of sugarcane), go in the garbage. Natural corks are too dense to compost. Synthetic corks are made of various materials and are not recyclable.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Corks can be dropped off for recycling at a handful of locations across Seattle via ReCORK .

items.explanation: CORKS go in the garbage or can be dropped off at stores in the Cork ReHarvest program, like PCC, Whole Foods and Wine World.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Receipts that are made of uncoated paper are recyclable. These are usually white or yellow, and are crisp and not shiny. You might receive a receipt like this from an antique store or yard sale.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Paper receipts that have grease stains from food can go in the compost container. Note that greasy coated receipts should never go in the compost, only garbage.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • The most common type of receipt, (coated/thermal,) always goes in the garbage. This is what you will commonly recieve from automatic printers at stores.

items.explanation: RECEIPTS can be recycled. Paper and thermal paper receipts go in the recycling.

items.synonyms: Corrugated Cardboard Cardboard Boxes Pizza Boxes

disposal_conditions.recycling: Cardboard and flattened cardboard boxes go in the recycling. Remove all packing materials from boxes.
disposal_conditions.compost: Cardboard with grease or oil stains (like pizza boxes) go in the food and yard waste cart. Put cardboard that is mostly wet in the food and yard waste cart. Remove all labels and tape.
disposal_conditions.special: Cardboard that doesn't fit can go next to your cart on your recycling day. There is no charge for extra recycling. Bundle and set next to the cart, or put in a box or container next to your cart.

items.explanation: CORRUGATED CARDBOARD goes in the recycling.  Flatten the boxes first.  Tape and labels are okay to leave on.  If the cardboard is wet or greasy then remove any  tape and labels and put the cardboard in the yard waste.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Dead animals weighing less than 15 pounds should be double-bagged and placed in your garbage can.

items.explanation: DEAD ANIMALS go in the garbage. Dead animals that are less than 15 pounds should be double bagged in the garbage.  To dispose of larger animals, call Animal Control (206) 386-7387

items.item_name: Deli, Coffee, or Soft Drink Lids
synonyms: Deli, Coffee, or Soft Drink Lids

disposal_conditions.recycling: Lids 3 inches in diameter or larger go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Lids less than 3 inches in diameter go in the garbage.

items.explanation: DELI, COFFEE, OR SOFT DRINK LIDS larger than 3 inches go in the recycling.   Smaller or dirty lids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Small amounts of dirt, soil, mud, or sand, including those contaminated with non-hazardous materials such as plastic, metal, and glass, can go in the garbage. Be careful not to exceed weight limits for your cart. Do not put dirt, soil, mud, or sand in the food and yard waste cart, as it can harm the composting process.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Contaminated soil (oil, fuel, heavy metals, etc.) must to be diposed of through an approved vendor. Go to HazWasteHelp.org for more information.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Larger amounts of clean dirt, soil, mud, or sand (free of trash and contamination) can be recycled by various landscaping supply companies in the area. Dirt or soil contaminated with non-hazardous materials such as metal, plastic, or glass can be brought to city transfer stations . Check rates here .

items.explanation: DIRT OR SOIL can go to the transfer station or go in the garbage. It is not accepted in the yard waste. If it contains hazardous materials, call the Household Hazards Line at 206-296-4692. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Put all disposable (even if marked "compostable") diapers in a bag, tie off, and place in garbage.

items.explanation: Put all disposable diapers in a bag, tie off, and place in the garbage, even if marked compostable.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Drinking glasses can go in the garbage, whether they're broken or not.

items.explanation: DRINKING GLASSES go in the garbage, whether they're broken or not. They are not accepted for recycling because they have different melting points than recyclable glass.

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Dryer sheets go in the garbage. They are made from polyester substrate and can't be recycled or composted. • Lint goes in the garbage. It is not recyclable nor compostable.

items.explanation: DRYER SHEETS go in the garbage.  They are made from polyester substrate and cannot be recycled or composted.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Home plastic plumbing, insulation, tar paper, caulking, silicone and wall material is garbage. Insulation material must be bagged. Garbage container lids must be able to close. Containers cannot exceed their weight limit .
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Check the King County What Do I Do With tool to find locations that will take building materials such as clean drywall and fiberglass . Construction material can be dropped off at one of Seattle's Transfer Stations for disposal. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Many household materials in good condition are salvageable. Check the Salvage Building Materials page and the Northwest Building Salvage Network to find more information about what materials can be salvaged and reused.

items.explanation: Drywall goes in the garbage or can be dropped off at City transfer stations for a fee. Clean drywall scraps should be recycled at construction and demolition facilities. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

items.item_name: Ducts, Furnace & Stove Parts, Fireplace Inserts

disposal_conditions.hazardous: To dispose of duct work, fire place inserts, or other materials - that contains asbestos, find disposal sites on the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency or call them at (206) 343-8800.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Many scrap metal dealers will buy asbestos-free metal duct, fireplace inserts and furnace/stove/chimney parts.

items.explanation: DUCTS, FURNACE & STOVE PARTS AND FIREPLACE INSERTS are potentially hazardous because these parts often contain asbestos or other hazardous materials and are not accepted at the transfer station.  Call Puget Sound Clean Air Agency for more information at (206) 343-8800 for disposal options.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Rigid plastic egg cartons that are empty, clean, and dry.
disposal_conditions.compost: Paper egg cartons are not a highly valuable recyclable; therefore, it is best to place them in the Food and Yard Waste container for composting.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Foam egg cartons are not recyclable nor compostable.

items.explanation: EGG CARTONS can go in yard waste or recycling.  Foam and dirty cartons go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Car parts 3 inches or smaller on any side can go in the garbage. • Car part accessories like windshield wipers or floor mats go in the garbage. • Snow chains should go in the garbage unless they are in good condition and can be donated.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Many scrap metal dealers will buy car parts.

items.explanation: ENGINES, MOTORS OR CAR PARTS go in the garbage or can be dropped off at City Transfer stations for a fee.  Many scrap metal dealers will buy car parts. Car parts aren't accepted for recycling because they contain grease, which contaminates other recyclable items. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Recycle all paper envelopes, including those with plastic windows.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Bubble pack, plastic, and mixed material envelopes go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Paper envelopes go in the recycling, including those with plastic windows. Bubble pack envelopes go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: E-Waste can't go in the garbage because they contain materials harmful to people, animals, and the environment.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • King County’s Take It Back Network is a network of e-cyclers that accept a variety of electronic equipment. Use their search tool to find the closest location to you. No fee for TVs, monitors, and computers covered from households and small businesses through the E-Cycle Washington program. Fees may apply for other items. • 1-800-RECYCLE is both a hotline and an online tool that connects you or your business to recycling services across Washington. This tool can help you find where in your community you can recycle everything from used appliances to leftover paint. Fees may apply.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a Special Item pickup. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate electronic equipment in working condition to local thrift stores or resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: E-WASTE can be dropped off for recycling or picked up at home for a fee. Find locations to drop off e-waste through the Take It Back Network.  To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000

disposal_conditions.garbage: Eyeglasses can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: You can donate eyeglasses to The Lions Club or check to see if where you got your glasses has a take back program.
disposal_conditions.donate: Contact your local thrift store to see if they take prescription glasses in good condition.

items.explanation: EYEGLASSES go in the garbage or can be dropped off for donation.  Find donation sites through The Lions Club or organizations affiliated with OneSight.

disposal_conditions.compost: 100% authentic, natural burlap (made from plant fibers, not polyester) is compostable. Cut it into strips and place in compost.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Fabric and textiles can go in the garbage. • Electric blankets, denim insulation, and cotton insulation go in the garbage, they are not able to be recycled through Threadcycle. • Plastic shower curtains go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate fabrics and textiles to thrift stores. Including stained, worn out, ripped, or damaged fabric or textiles can be turned into new products through Threadcycle . Some local thrift and second-hand stores are partners and will accept these items at their regular collection or drop box locations. • Donate plastic shower curtains in good condition to local thrift stores.

items.explanation: FABRIC OR TEXTILES go in the garbage or can be dropped off for recycling at thrift stores in the Threadcycle program.  Find locations at Kingcounty.gov/threadcycle

disposal_conditions.recycling: You can recycle up to 2 gallons of cooking oil on each recycling collection day: • Only cooking oil that is liquid at room temperature is recyclable. • Pour cooled oil into a 1-gallon plastic container with a tight-fitting, screw-on lid. • Set up to 2 labeled 1-gallon containers on the curb next to your recycling cart. • Apartment / Condo residents: Set out up to 2 labeled 1-gallon containers per cart at your building/complex.
disposal_conditions.compost: Small amounts of oil and grease can be wiped up by paper towels or newspaper and placed in the food and yard waste container (compost).
disposal_conditions.garbage: Put cooled fats, oil, or grease in a closed container, Ziploc bag, or in a thick plastic bag that's tightly tied.
disposal_conditions.special: You can recycle up to 2 gallons of cooking oil on each recycling collection day: • Pour cooled oil into a 1-gallon plastic container with a tight-fitting, screw-on lid. • Label the container with your name and address. • Set up to 2 labeled 1-gallon containers on the curb next to your recycling cart. • Apartment / Condo residents: Set out up to 2 labeled 1-gallon containers per cart at your building/complex.

items.explanation: FATS, OILS, OR GREASE go in the garbage in a closed container. Never pour down the drain because they clog pipes and create backups.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Fiberglass can go in the garbage: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.donate: Bring fiberglass to city transfer stations . Fees apply.

items.explanation: FIBERGLASS goes in the garbage. It can also be dropped off at City Transfer stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty CO2 and halon (bromo difluoromethane) fire extinguishers go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring fire extinguishers that are full or partially full to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee.

items.explanation: FIRE EXTINGUISHERS go in the garbage. Empty CO2 and halon (bromo difluoromethane) fire extinguishers go in the garbage.   Bring fire extinguishers that are full or partially full to a household hazardous waste facility for free. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: BBQ briqettes and fireplace and briquette ashes must be completely cooled, wetted, and placed in a closed box or bag. This prevents ash from scattering when your garbage is collected.

items.explanation: FIREPLACE OR BRIQUETTE ASHES goes in the garbage. Briquettes and ashes must be completely cooled, wetted, and placed in a closed box or bag. This prevents ash from scattering when your garbage is collected.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Only fireworks that are completely burned out AND cooled down by submerging them in a bucket of water for fifteen minutes can be put in the garbage. DO NOT put used fireworks that haven't been soaked into a paper or plastic bag, or unused fireworks in the garbage. This could catch fire in the bag. If disposed of improperly, used fireworks can pose a danger to employees who handle trash at local transfer stations.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Call the Seattle Police Firing Range at (206) 684-7470 to dispose of small, unused residential fireworks.

items.explanation: FIREWORKS go in the garbage. Make sure used fireworks are completely burned out and cooled down by submerging them in a bucket of water for fifteen minutes before putting them in the garbage. Learn more on the Washington State Fire Marshal website.   To dispose of unused fireworks, Call the Seattle Police Firing Range at (206) 684-7470 for more information.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring up to 10 flares per trip to a household hazardous waste facility for no fee. Some limits may apply. Call the King County Hazardous Waste Help Line at (206) 296-4692 for more information before dropping off your flares.

items.explanation: Bring up to 10 flares per trip to a household hazardous waste facility for no fee. Some limits may apply. Call the King County Hazardous Waste Help Line at (206) 296-4692 for more information before dropping off your flares.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Incandecent, LED, Halogen, LED commercial downlights and Xenon light bulbs go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring up to 10 fluorescent tubes and CFLs per trip to a household hazardous waste facility or to a LightRecycle location. No fee.
disposal_conditions.special: For Compact Fluorescent bulbs ONLY: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup for a $5 fee.

items.explanation: FLUORESCENT LIGHT BULBS are potentially hazardous.  Take them to a household hazardous waste facility, or LightRecycle.org locations for free. Or schedule a pick up at your home for a $5 fee. To prevent breaking the tubes or bulbs and releasing mercury when storing or transporting, wrap them in newspaper or put back in their original packaging. Fluorescent tubes and CFLs are banned from the garbage because they contain mercury, which is highly toxic. To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206) 684-3000

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Baby wipes, wet wipes, cleaning wipes, and any wipes labeled "flushable" go in your garbage. If flushed down your toilet, they can clog your sewer pipes. • Single use wipes labelled as compostable should still go in the garbage, as Seattle only accepts compostable serviceware outside of organics.

items.explanation: FLUSHABLE WIPES go in the garbage. This includes Baby wipes, wet wipes, cleaning wipes, and any wipes labeled "flushable".   If flushed down your toilet, they can clog your sewer pipes leading to backups.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Foamcore and foam boards go in the garbage.

items.explanation: FOAMCORE and foamboards go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic liners, bags, waxed bags, and trays from food boxes, such as cereal or crackers, go in the garbage.

items.explanation: FOOD BOX LINERS go in the garbage. Plastic liners, bags, and trays are too dirty to recycle.

disposal_conditions.compost: • Fruit and vegetables (including pits and seeds) • Bread, pasta, rice, and grains (cooked and uncooked) • Eggshells and nutshells • Coffee grounds, filters, and tea bags • Meat, fish, and chicken • Dairy products like milk, butter, and cheese • Flour, sugar • Protein powder • Bones and seafood shells • Compostable (uncoated) paper such as paper napkins, paper towels, and pizza boxes • Spoiled or moldy food
disposal_conditions.garbage: Tea bags that are made of plastic go in the garbage

items.explanation: FOOD SCRAPS go in the yard waste.  This includes meat, dairy, bones and shells.

disposal_conditions.compost: Uncoated paper with food or grease on it goes in the food and yard waste. This includes: • Pizza boxes • Paper napkins • Paper towels used to wipe up food • Uncoated (not shiny) paper plates and bowls (such as Chinet brand) • compostable straws and utensils • wet newspaper
disposal_conditions.garbage: Shiny, plastic-coated food packaging and fast food wrappers, paper plates, or bowls go in the garbage. Butcher paper and aluminum foil food wrappers belong in the garbage. They are made of multiple materials and are hard to properly clean for recycling.

items.explanation: FOOD-SOILED PAPER goes in the yard waste.  Make sure the paper does not have a shiny coating, which is plastic and non compostable.  Waxed paper, where you can scratch the wax off, is acceptable.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Clean frozen food boxes and other food boxes • Clean, empty and dry trays that hold the food • Plastic coated cardboard and paperboard
disposal_conditions.garbage: Food trays that are not clean empty and dry go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Clean FROZEN FOOD BOXES can be recycled.   Dirty, food covered boxes go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: To dispose furnaces, furnace parts, or boilers that contain asbestos, contact the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency at (206) 343-8800.

items.explanation: FURNACES are a health hazard. Due to their potentially hazardous nature, they are not accepted at the transfer stations. Many scrap metal dealers will buy asbestos-free metal duct and furnace parts. To dispose of furnaces, furnace parts, or boilers that contain asbestos, contact the Puget Sound Clean Air Agency at (206) 343-8800.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Hoses go in the garbage. Cut hose into pieces if it doesn't fit in your cart with the lid closed.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give away a gently used hose using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing . You could also consider contacting your local P-patch community garden to donate a gently used hose.

items.explanation: GARDEN HOSES go in the garbage.  Do not put them in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty fuel cans that are 5 gallons or smaller can go in the garbage. Leave lid off empty can for 2 days before putting in garbage to release fuel fumes.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Small fuel canisters (5 gallons or less) with any amount of gas, diesel, kerosene, butane or other fuel can be taken to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee.

items.explanation: Empty GAS CANS that are 5 gallons or smaller can go in the garbage.   Leave the lid off empty cans for 2 days to releasse gas fumes before putting in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring fuel to a household hazardous waste facility : • Put fuel in a sealed container that is 5 gallons or less. • Use fuel-compatible containers (not food or milk jugs). • Limit 30 gallons per trip.

items.explanation: GASOLINE is a health hazard. Bring gasoline to a household hazardous waste facility for free. Limit 30-gallons per trip.  Put gas in sealed containers that are 5 gallons or less. Use approved gasoline containers (not food or milk jugs). For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: Gel hot and cold packs go in the garbage. They are not recyclable or compostable. Gel packs labeled 'Compostable' are not accepted in food and yard Waste carts. Do Not pour gel down the drain or flush down the toilet. It will clog the pipes.

items.explanation: GEL PACKS goes in the garbage. They are not recyclable or compostable.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Wrapping paper • Paper gift bags • Paper gift boxes • Gift tissue paper • Kraft Paper • Construction Paper • Inner cardboard tube • Clean, and dry wrapping paper go into recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: These items go in the garbage: • Plastic gift wrap • Foil gift wrap • Plastic or foil gift bows • Plastic gift bags

items.explanation: GIFT WRAP can be recycled if it is wrapping paper, Paper gift bags, Paper gift boxes, Gift tissue paper.  But it goes in the garbage if it is  Plastic gift wrap, Foil gift wrap, Plastic or foil gift bows.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Glass bottles and jars larger than 3 inches. • Lids 3 inches or larger
disposal_conditions.garbage: Bottles smaller than three inches, any frosted glass bottles, Pyrex, and any heat-treated bakeware or kitchen glass is not recyclable.

items.explanation: GLASS BOTTLES OR JARS go in the recycling.  Bottles and jars must be Empty, Clean and Dry. Lids 3 inches or larger can be recycled.  Lids smaller than 3-inches can be recycled only if attached to the bottle or jar.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Damaged glass or ceramic household items such as plates, bowls, drinking glasses, or terra cotta pots can go in the garbage. Any frosted glass, picture frame glass, glass used for candles, heat-treated bakeware or kitchen glass is garbage. These types of glass are not recyclable
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate glass or ceramic household items in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: You can donate GLASS OR CERAMIC HOUSEHOLD ITEMS in good condition to local thrift stores, on Craigslist, and Buy Nothing group or it can go in the garbage.  Damaged glass or ceramic household items such as plates, bowls, or drinking glasses can go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Non-toxic glues go in the garbage. This includes Elmer's, glue sticks, and other glues used in schools.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring glues and adhesives labeled with caution , warning , danger , or poison to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. • All containers must be 5 gallons or less. • Limit 50 gallons per trip.

items.explanation: GLUES OR ADHESIVES go in the garbage. Non-toxic glues go in the garbage. This includes Elmer's, glue sticks, and other glues used in schools.   Bring glues and adhesives labeled with caution, warning, danger, or poison to a household hazardous waste facility for free. All containers must be 5 gallons or less. Limit 50-gallons per trip. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: You can put gold, silver, and other precious metals in the garbage. It is not a material that can be collected in large enough amouts for recycling.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: You can often sell gold, silver, and broken jewelry to jewelry buyers or coin and precious metal dealers.

items.explanation: GOLD, SILVER and other precious metals go in the garbage or you can often sell gold, silver, and broken jewelry to jewelry buyers or coin and precious metal dealers.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Many charitable organizations accept vehicles and provide a receipt for a tax deduction.

items.explanation: VEHICLES must be picked up for disposal and recycling.  Many local charities will take vehicles as a donation.

disposal_conditions.compost: Grass clippings and small amounts of sod can go in the food and yard waste. Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring large amounts of grass clippings and sod to city transfer stations . Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Consider leaving grass clippings in place so the nutrients can enrich the lawn. Check the Garden Hotline to learn about grasscycling.

items.explanation: GRASS CLIPPINGS OR SOD goes in the yard waste or can be taken to City Transfer stations for a fee.  Remove as much soil as possible from the sod. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Greeting cards and other paper cards go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Cards with lots of plastic, glitter, or other materials: remove the non-paper parts and put in the garbage.

items.explanation: Paper GREETING CARDS go in the recycling.

synonyms: Produce Bags or Bulk Food Bags

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean and dry paper bags
disposal_conditions.compost: Paper bags that are wet or food-soiled
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Plastic bags that are wet or food-soiled • Plastic produce bags • Single plastic bags
disposal_conditions.dropoff: You can return clean plastic grocery bags and other plastic films to participating grocery stores to be recycled. Use a lookup tool to find locations near you.
disposal_conditions.donate: Reuse bags in good condition

items.explanation: Plastic GROCERY OR SHOPPING BAGS go in the garbage.  The City of Seattle no longer accepts plastic bags for recycle, but if they are clean and dry then plastic bags can be taken to retail locations for recycling.  Clean paper bags can go in recycling, but wet or food soiled paper bags go in yard waste.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring gutters 8 feet or less in length to city transfer stations : • Fees apply for plastic gutters. • There is no fee for aluminum gutters.

items.explanation: GUTTERS can go to the transfer station, fees may apply. Gutters should be less than 8 feet long. Aluminum gutters can be recycled for free. Plastic gutters are disposed of for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Mainly metal tools 3 inches or more in size • Can include small amounts of wood, rubber, or plastic
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal tools less than 3 inches • Tools that contain large amounts of rubber, wood, or plastic
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate hand tools in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Donate hand tools in good condition to local thrift stores, on Craigslist, and Buy Nothing groups. Metal tools larger than 3 inches can be put in the recycling cart.  They can include small amounts of wood, rubber, or plastic   Smaller and tools with multiple materials go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: If you remove the hard cover and binding, the inside pages can be recycled.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Intact (with cover and binding) hardcover books go in the garbage. This is because the hard cover and binding are not recyclable.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate hardbound books in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: HARDCOVER BOOKS go in the garbage.  Hardcover books cannot be recycled due to the glue in the binding.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty helium tanks 5 gallons or smaller can go in the garbage: • Turn the valve all the way to the left to release remaining helium. • Avoid extra charges: Tank should fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Check with your supplier for return and refill options. For tanks larger than 5 gallons or commercial cylinders, refer to information printed on the tank for instructions on recycling or exchange options.

items.explanation: HELIUM TANKS go in the garbage or can be dropped off at City Transfer stations. Empty helium tanks 5 gallons or smaller can go in the garbage. Turn the valve all the way to the left to release remaining helium.  Check with your supplier for return and refill options. For tanks larger than 5 gallons or commercial cylinders, refer to information printed on the tank for instructions on recycling or exchange options.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty hobby chemical containers go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring partially full containers of hobby chemicals labeled with caution , warning , danger , or poison to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. • All containers must be 5 gallons or less. • Limit 50 gallons per trip.

items.explanation: Empty HOBBY CHEMICALS go in the garbage.  Bring partially full containers of hobby chemicals labeled with caution, warning, danger, or poison to a household hazardous waste facility for free. All containers must be 5 gallons or less.  Limit 50-gallons per trip. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: String/Holiday lights can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Check King County's Green Holiday website for drop off locations for holiday light recycling options. • You can drop off your solar garden lights at EcoLight for recycling.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate holiday lights in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: HOLIDAY LIGHTS go in the garbage. During the holiday season some stores will take them back for recycling.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Empty and dry paper cups. • Paper cup sleeves go into recycling. • Plastic to-go cups
disposal_conditions.compost: • Hot or coffee cups labeled as "Compostable" • Some plastic-looking to-go cups may be compostable! Look for the word "compostable" and a certification.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Styrofoam coffee cups • Non-compostable paper cups stained with liquid • Non-paper cup sleeves • Plastic straws

items.explanation: HOT OR TO-GO BEVERAGE CUPS that are empty, clean and dry can go in the recycling.  Dirty cups or Styrofoam  cups go in the garbage.  Compostable cups go in the yard waste.

disposal_conditions.compost: Houseplants go in the food and yard waste. Remove plants from potting soil and planters.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Houseplants can go to city transfer stations as yard waste. Remove soil and pots. Fees apply. Check rates here .

items.explanation: HOUSEPLANTS go in the yard waste. Remove the soil and the pot before putting it in the yard waste.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Metal furniture frames go into recycle.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • You can bring furniture and large frames to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page . • If you have a piece of furniture that would fit in your garbage cart, you can dispose of it at home with you household garbage. It must fit in the cart. Do not exceed weight limits.
disposal_conditions.special: • Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup. Fees apply. • If you are a resident of an apartment or a condominium and do not have an SPU account, check your property manager. Furniture is not allowed in dumpsters.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate furniture in good condition to local thrift stores . • Resell or give away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing . • Unwanted light fixtures or other household wares in working condition can be donated to salvage stores. Learn more about deconstruction and find your local salvage retailer (under reuse) on our salvage webpage .

items.explanation: HOUSEHOLD FURNITURE can be donated or resold if it is in good working condition.  Contact thrift stores to see if it is accepted.  Broken or large frames can be taken to Seattle transfer stations or picked up for a fee. To schedule a pickup go to www.seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206) 684-3000

disposal_conditions.recycling: Paper, plastic, and plastic coated paper ice cream cartons and lids go in the recycling if they are clean, dry, and empty.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Dirty ice cream cartons go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Empty, Clean and Dry ICE CREAM CARTONS go in the recycling.  Dirty containers go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Metal parts larger than 3 inches go in the recycling. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached. • Rusted metal can be recycled unless the metal is falling apart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items with large amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached. • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: All metal, except car parts, is accepted as recycling at city transfer stations . There is no fee for most metal. • Maximum size is 8 feet long. • There is a charge for appliances, limit 2 per load.
disposal_conditions.special: For larger items, submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item collection. Fees apply. Large metal parts can be put at the curb for pickup on your recycling collection day. • These must be bundled or tied. • The maximum size is 2 feet on all sides.

items.explanation: IRON OR STEEL can be recycled. Pieces larger than 3 inches without large amounts of rubber, plastic or wood go in the recycling.  You can drop off iron and steel at City Transfer stations for recycling for free. Large items can be picked up for a fee. Schedule a pick up at www.seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: You can put jewelry and coins in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: You can donate jewelry and coins to thrift stores, and can often sell these items to jewelry buyers or coin and precious metal dealers.

items.explanation: You can donate jewelry and coins to thrift stores, and can often sell these items to jewelry buyers or coin and precious metal dealers. You can put jewelry and coins in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: All paper junk mail goes in the recycling. Please open and separate junk mail before recycling. Items include: • Catalogs • Flyers • Envelopes with or without clear plastic windows • Newspapers • Phone books • Magazines • Glossy paper

items.explanation: JUNK MAIL goes in the recycling this includes all paper junk mail,  magazines, and catalogs.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Keys and other metal items smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Keys and other metal items smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.   They cannot be recycled because small metal items can fall through equipment at the recycling sorting facility.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Kleenex (facial tissue) goes in the garbage.

items.explanation: KLEENEX and FACIAL TISSUE go in the garbage.

items.item_name: Latex Paint, Acrylic Paint, and Water-Based Paint

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Dry latex, water-based, or acrylic paint can go in the trash. Follow these steps to get rid of it safely: • If there is less than an inch of paint in the can, remove the lid and air dry; protected from children and pets. • If there is more than an inch of paint in the can, mix the paint with cat litter and let it solidify. You can also use a paint hardener according to directions. • Separate and throw the can of dried paint and lid in the garbage. Do not take the paint out of the can. • Empty paint cans cannot be cleaned enough to recycle and should go in the garbage. • Paint brushes with dried paint can go into the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Residential customers with unlabeled paint can bring it to a Household Hazardous Waste location for identification and guidance on disposal options. HHW accepts oil-based paints (for free) and can advise customers on locations to take latex paints.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Recycle your paint products for free at a PaintCare drop-off site. Accepted products include latex paint, oil-based paint, stain, varnish, and more. Visit the PaintCare website to find a drop-off location near you and to view a list of accepted products. You can also recycle unwanted latex paint, water-based stains, and clear finishes at any Take it Back Network paint recycling location . There is a fee for this service.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Check with local community organizations or schools to see if they will accept water-based paint or acrylic paint. • Clean paint brushes can be donated to local thrift stores

items.explanation: LATEX PAINT goes in the garbage or can be dropped off for recycling at specific facilities for a fee.  Dry out the latex paint by leaving the can open or adding cat litter.  Separate the lid from the dried paint can and put both in your garbage (removing the lid lets your garbage collector knows the paint is dried).  Drop off for a fee at local locations in the Take it Back network.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Empty and flatten laundry detergent boxes and then put in the recycling.

items.explanation: LAUNDRY DETERGENT BOXES go in the recycling. Empty and flatten laundry detergent boxes and then put in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Plastic laundry detergent jugs that are clean, dry, and empty. • Put plastic caps back onto the bottle for recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Loose caps go in the garbage.

items.explanation: LAUNDRY DETERGENT JUGS go in the recycling. Make sure the container is empty, screw the cap back on and put in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: • Bring gas and oil to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. • Don't mix fluids together! • Put fluids in a sealed container that is 5 gallons or less. • Limit 50 gallons per trip.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • You must drain all gas, oil, and air from lawn mowers, air compressors, and other power equipment. • Bring emptied mowers and equipment to city transfer stations . • No charge for lawn mowers.
disposal_conditions.donate: • If in working condition, consider donating tools to your local tool library. There are locations in North , South , and West Seattle . • Donate lawn mowers or power equipment in working condition to local thrift stores . Call first to confirm acceptance policies. • Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: There are two options.  You can donate lawn mowers or power equipment in working condition to local thrift stores. Call first to confirm acceptance policies.   Another option is to bring emptied mowers and equipment to city transfer stations, but you must first drain all the gas and oil. There is no charge for lawn mowers.  For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations. Bring gas and oil to a household hazardous waste facility for free. Don't mix fluids together!
