Put fluids in a sealed container that is 5 gallons or less.  Limit 50 gallons per trip. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.recycling: Lead pieces 3 inches or larger can go in the recycling: • Lead should fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring lead 8 feet or smaller to city transfer stations . Fees apply.

items.explanation: You can put lead pieces 3 inches or larger in your recycling.  Pieces 8 feet or smaller can be dropped off at a city transfer stations for a fee.  Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your bin.

items.item_name: Leaves, seeds, or Non-Woody Yard Waste

disposal_conditions.compost: Leaves, seeds, and non-woody yard waste go in the food and yard waste. • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart. From November 1-30, customers with Food & Yard Waste service can put out up to 10 bags of extra yard waste for free per collection day.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring leaves, seeds, or non-woody yard waste to city transfer stations . Fees apply .
disposal_conditions.special: Put extra yard waste in kraft paper, labeled "compostable" bags, or bundled with natural twine for pick up on collection day. Fees apply .

items.explanation: LEAVES OR NON-WOODY YARD WASTE goes in food and yard waste or can be dropped off at city transfer stations for a fee. Leaves and non-woody yard waste go in the food and yard waste. Items must fit in your bin with the lid closed to avoid extra fees.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Remove the tabs and bend the license plates before putting in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring license plates to a vehicle licensing office or mail them to: Department of Licensing 1125 Washington St. SE Olympia, WA 98504

items.explanation: LICENSE PLATES can be recycled.  First remove the tabs and bend the license plates before putting in the recycling.  They can also be dropped off at a vehicle licensing office, or you can mail them to the Department of Licensing at 1125 Washington Street  Southeast,  Olympia, Washington.  The zipcode is 98504.

disposal_conditions.compost: Unpainted and untreated wood goes in the food and yard waste: • All pieces must be less than 4 inches in diameter and under 4 feet long. • Remove nails and all other non-wood materials. • Break lumber and boards down so they fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Do not exceed weight limits posted on your cart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Painted or treated wood goes in the garbage: • All pieces must be less than 4 inches in diameter and under 4 feet long. • Break lumber and boards down so they fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Do not exceed weight limits .
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring lumber and wood boards to city transfer stations . {{proj[1].rate_text}}
disposal_conditions.special: Lumber or boards, that are painted or treated, which do not fit in your cart with the lid closed may be put in a plastic bag and placed next to your garbage cart on collection day. {{proj[4].rate_text}}

items.explanation: LUMBER OR BOARDS can go to the transfer station for a fee.  Painted and treated wood goes in the garbage. All pieces must be less than 4 inches in diameter and under 4 feet long.  Clean wood goes in food and yard waste. Break lumber and boards down so they fit in your bin with the lid closed.  Remove nails and all other non-wood materials. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Magazines go in the recycling.

items.explanation: MAGAZINES go in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring mattresses, mattress toppers and futons to city transfer stations for disposal. Fees apply. Check rates here .
disposal_conditions.special: Schedule a Special Item pickup online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate futon frames in good condition to local thrift stores . Mattresses and futons are generally not accepted at thrift stores.

items.explanation: MATTRESSES, MATTRESS TOPPERS OR FUTONS can be placed at curb if you schedule a pick up for a fee.  Go to  www.seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206) 684-3000.   You can also drop them off at City Transfer Stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean rigid plastic meat trays go in the recycling. Remove the plastic absorbent insert before recycling.
disposal_conditions.compost: Brown or tan trays for meat, poultry, and fish are made of compostable material and go in the food and yard waste.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Any other color of foam meat tray and the plastic absorbent insert go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Styrofoam meat trays and the plastic absorbent insert go in the garbage.   Brown or beige trays for meat, poultry, and fish are made from plant-based material and are compostable so they can go in the food and yard waste.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Pill bottles, such as aspirin or vitamin bottles, go in the recycling. These are often white, clear, or colors other than orange.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Clear plastic prescription medicine vials go the garbage. These are often orange or brown. These are made from a different type of plastic that is too brittle to recycle. • Plastic medicine bottles smaller than 3 inches should go in the garbage. • If you are unable to thourougly clean bottles like cough syrup bottles, they should also go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring medicines to a secure medicine return site .

items.explanation: Pill bottles, such as aspirin or vitamin bottles, go in the recycling.   Plastic orange prescription medicine vials go the garbage.   To find a location to get rid of medications go to med dash project dot org

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring medicines to a secure medicine return site. Find locations here .

items.explanation: Drop off unused medicine. Find a location at med dash project dot org.   Flushing medicine down the toilet or sink, or putting medicine in your backyard compost or food and yard waste bin can pollute our waterways and the environment.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Metal sinks and fixtures go in the recycling: • They must be between 3 inches and 2 feet on all sides. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Metal sinks can be dropped off at metal recyclers, often for free. Bring metal sinks, tubs, and fixtures less than 8 feet on all sides to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check rates here .
disposal_conditions.special: Schedule a Special Item Pickup online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: You can often donate or resell sinks, tubs, and fixtures to building supply stores that specialize in salvage materials. Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: You can donate or resell sinks, tubs, and fixtures to building supply stores that specialize in salvage materials, on Craigslist, or Buy Nothing groups. A second options is that Metal sinks and fixtures larger than 3 inches can go in the recycling. Another option is to schedule a pick up for a $30 fee at  www.seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000.   You can also bring metal sinks, tubs, and fixtures less than 8 feet on all sides to city transfer stations. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.compost: Any leftover popcorn or kernels go in the food and yard waste.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Microwave popcorn bags go in the garbage.

items.explanation: MICROWAVE POPCORN BAGS go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Paper and plastic milk, juice, and soup cartons when they are clean, empty, and dry • Cartons labeled Tetra Pak • Other aseptic cartons Plastic spouts don't need to be removed to recycle food cartons.

items.explanation: MILK, JUICE, OR SOUP CARTONS can be recycled. They need to be Empty, Clean and Dry. Plastic spouts don't need to be removed to recycle milk cartons.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Mirrors can go in the garbage: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring large mirrors that don't fit in the cart to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check rates here .
disposal_conditions.special: Schedule a Special Item Pickup online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate mirrors in working condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing . Some building supply or second use stores accept mirrors.

items.explanation: Donate mirrors in working condition to local thrift stores, on Craigslist, and Buy Nothing groups. Some building supply or second use stores accept mirrors.   Mirrors can go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Put small amounts of construction waste in the garbage. Do not exceed weight limits.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: For other disposal and recycling options, view hauling requirements for certified facilities . Bring mixed construction and demolition waste to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check rates here .

items.explanation: Mixed construction and demolition materials can go to a transfer station or be taken to a construction and demolition facility.  For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty motor oil containers and filters go in the garbage. They can't be recycled. Puncture the oil filter and drain the oil into a container for 24 hours. Seal drained filter in a plastic bag and put in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring contaminated oil that contains solvents, bleach, or other materials to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring partially full motor oil containers to: • City transfer stations . Fees apply. • A household hazardous waste facility . No fee. Bring drained or full oil filters to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. Bring up to 5 gallons of oil per a trip to city transfer stations . No fee.
disposal_conditions.special: You can recycle up to 2 gallons (or TWO 5 quart oil product containers) of motor oil on each recycling collection day: • Pour cooled oil into a plastic container with a tight-fitting, screw-on lid. • Label the container with your name and address. • Set up to 2 labeled containers on the curb next to your recycling cart. • Apartment / Condo residents: Set out up to 2 labeled containers per cart at your building/complex.

items.explanation: Motor oil can be recycled if it doesn't contain solvents, bleach, or other materials.    Set out up to 2 labeled 1 gallon plastic containers with lids next to your recycling bin for free.  Or, it can go to the transfer station.  Bring up to 5 gallons of oil per a trip to city transfer stations for free.  You can also bring contaminated oil that contains solvents, bleach, or other materials to a household hazardous waste facility for free.  Never pour motor oil on the ground or down the drain; it can pollute our waterways and the environment.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Put broken mugs, plates, and bowls in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate dinnerware in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: MUGS, PLATES OR BOWLS in good condition can be donated to thrift stores, on Craigslist and Buy Nothing groups. Broken items go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Newspaper goes in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.compost: Wet or food-soiled newspaper goes in the food and yard waste.

items.explanation: NEWSPAPERS can be recycled or go in food and yard waste. Wet or food-soiled newspaper goes in the food and yard waste.   Clean, recyclable newspaper is not allowed in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Small office supplies, such as binders, pencil sharpeners, paper clips, rubber bands, pens, and pencils can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring office furniture and equipment to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate furniture, equipment, and supplies in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: OFFICE FURNITURE AND EQUIPMENT can be donated or resold if in good working condition.  Contact thrift stores to see if they are accepted.  Broken or large items can be taken to Seattle transfer stations or picked up for a fee. To schedule a pickup go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206) 684-3000

disposal_conditions.hazardous: To learn how to dispose of oil tanks, call our household hazards line at (206) 296-4692. Hours are 9:00 a.m. to 4:30 p.m., Monday through Friday.

items.explanation: OIL TANKS are hazardous. To find out how to dispose of oil tanks, For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty oil-based paint cans can go in the garbage: • Remove the lid and let fumes evaporate outside, away from children and animals. • Separate the lid from the dried paint can and put both in your garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring oil-based paint to a Household Hazardous Waste facility . No fee. Residential customers with unlabeled paint can bring it to a Household Hazardous Waste (HHW) location for identification and guidance on disposal options. HHW accepts oil-based paints and can advise customers on locations to take latex paints.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Recycle your paint products for free at a PaintCare drop-off site. Accepted products include latex paint, oil-based paint, stain, varnish, and more. Visit the PaintCare website to find a drop-off location near you and to view a list of accepted products.

items.explanation: Empty and dry oil-based paint cans can go in the garbage. Remove the lid and let fumes evaporate outside, away from children and animals. Separate the lid from the dried paint can and put both in your garbage.   Or, Bring oil-based paint to a household hazardous waste facility for free. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring large appliances to a city transfer station. Fees apply. Limit of two large appliances per load.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup for large appliances. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Appliances in working condition can be resold using sites like Craigslist and Buy Nothing groups   To schedule a pick up for a fee, go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems. or call (206)684-3000 . You can drop off  up to 2 residential appliances per visit to city transfer stations. .. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring automotive fluids (such as brake, transmission, hydraulic, or power steering fluids) to a household hazardous waste facility : • Put fluids in a sealed container that is 5 gallons or less. • Limit 50 gallons per trip.

items.explanation: VEHICLE FLUIDS could be hazardous.  Bring automotive fluids (such as brake, transmission, hydraulic, or power steering fluids) to a household hazardous waste facility for free. Don't mix fluids with used motor oil!   Put fluids in a sealed container that is 5 gallons or less.  Limit 50-gallons per trip. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Put packing peanuts in a plastic bag and tie closed. Then put in the garbage. Corn starch peanuts, dissolvable peanuts, biodegradable peanuts, or any packing peanuts labeled in a way that suggest they may breakdown naturally, go in the garbage. They are not approved for the food and yard waste container. Watch Ask Evelyn Live to see how you can dissolve these packing peanuts in a container with water at your home.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Styro Recycle in Kent accepts Styrofoam blocks, packing sheets, and packing peanuts for recycling.

items.explanation: PACKING PEANUTS go in the garbage. Or can be dropped off for reuse.  Bag loose peanuts to put in the garbage.  UPS and FedEx stores will take them back for free.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty latex or oil-based paint cans can go in the garbage: • Remove the lid and let fumes evaporate outside, away from children and animals. • Separate the lid from the dried paint can and put both in your garbage.

items.explanation: LATEX PAINT goes in the garbage or can be dropped off for recycling at specific facilities for a fee.  Dry out the latex paint by leaving the can open or adding cat litter.  Separate the lid from the dried paint can and put both in your garbage. Or,drop off latex paint for a fee at local locations in the Take it Back network.      If this is an oil paint can,  Empty and dry oil-based paint cans can go in the garbage but cannot be recycled. Remove the lid and let fumes evaporate outside, away from children and animals. Separate the lid from the dried paint can and put both in your garbage.   Or, Bring oil-based paint to a household hazardous waste facility for free. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: Painted or treated wood, including creosote and railroad ties, can go in the garbage: • Break wood down so it fits in your cart with the lid closed. • Make sure it doesn't exceed the weight limits for your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring painted or treated lumber, including creosote and railroad ties, to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.

items.explanation: PAINTED OR TREATED WOOD goes in the garbage. You can also take it to City transfer stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.compost: Break pallets made from untreated wood into pieces that are: • 4 inches or less in diameter • 4 feet or less in length • Remove any nails or metallic items.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Pallets made from treated wood can be broken down and put in the garbage: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits for your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring pallets to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.special: These may be set out next to your garbage cart on collection day. Extra garbage fees apply.

items.explanation: Wood pallets without painted ends or chemical treatments can go in the yard waste, if they are broken down to fit in the cart.  If set out they will be charged as extra yard waste.  Bring to transfer station to dispose of as yard waste (if  it is clean wood), or dispose of as garbage (if it's treated or has painted ends).

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean and dry paper bags go the recycling. Stickers, tape, and staples okay.
disposal_conditions.compost: Food-soiled bags go in the food and yard waste cart. Please remove all stickers, tape, or staples.

items.explanation: Clean and dry paper bags go into recycling.   Food-soiled bags go in the food and yard waste cart.

disposal_conditions.compost: Paper berry baskets go in the food and yard waste.

items.explanation: PAPER BERRY BASKETS go in the food and yard waste.

disposal_conditions.compost: Paper napkins and towels that have food or grease on them go in the food and yard waste.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Paper napkins and towels used with cleaning products or that have bodily fluids go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Try to save clean and unused napkins for future use. Pro Tip: Place clean napkins in your vehicle’s glove compartment for emergency clean-ups.

items.explanation: PAPER NAPKINS go in the food and yard waste. Paper napkins and towels used with cleaning products or that have bodily fluids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Paper cups that are clean, empty, and dry • Clean paper plates Most paper cups have a thin plastic liner, even with this they are recyclable.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Paper plates or cups labeled as "Compostable" • Uncoated (not shiny) paper plates (such as Chinet brand)
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Dirty plates and cups with a shiny coating • Styrofoam plates and cups

items.explanation: Paper plates and cups that are clean, or with a small amount of liquid, can be reycled.   If they are labeled as "Compostable" or are Uncoated (not shiny)  then they go in food and yard waste.   Dirty plates and cups with a shiny coating, along with Styrofoam plates and cups, go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.compost: Paper punch holes go in the food and yard waste.

items.explanation: Paper punch holes are too small to be recycled at the recycling facility so they go in food and yard waste.

items.item_name: Paper Towel Tubes, Toilet Paper Tubes, Cardboard Tubes

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Paper towel and toilet paper tubes go in the recycling. Glue and small amounts of paper are okay to leave on. • Large cardboard tubes can also be recycled.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • The white plastic caps that fit into the ends of large cardboard tubes should go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Paper towel and toilet paper tubes go in the recycling.  Glue and small amounts of paper are okay to leave on.

disposal_conditions.compost: Paper towels and napkins that have food or grease on them go in the food and yard waste. Unused paper towels also go in the compost. The paper fiber is too short to be recycled into new paper products.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Paper towels used with cleaning products or that have bodily fluids on them and blue "shop" towels, which are made of cotton, go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Try to save clean and unused paper towels for future use.

items.explanation: PAPER TOWELS go in the food and yard waste. Paper napkins and towels used with cleaning products or that have bodily fluids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Paperback books can go in your recycling cart.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate paperback books in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Paperback books in good condition can be donated. Or they can go in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Jars can go in the recycling if they are clean, empty, and dry. If they are coated with oily food like peanut butter or mayonnaise, try washing with a little bit of hot water and soap. Then rinse jar clean. You could also wash them in the dishwasher.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Jars coated with oily film that cannot be cleaned off should to go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Empty, Clean and Dry PEANUT BUTTER OR MAYONNAISE JARS go in the recycling. Jars with food or oily film that cannot be cleaned off should to go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty pesticide containers go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring full or partially-full pesticide containers to your nearest household hazardous waste facility . No fee.

items.explanation: PESTICIDE CONTAINERS go in the garbage.   Bring full or partially-full pesticide containers to household hazardous waste facilities for free.   Find ways to avoid using pesticides from the Garden Hotline. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty pesticide containers go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring pesticides to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee.

items.explanation: PESTICIDES are hazardous. Bring pesticides to a household hazardous waste facility for free.   Find ways to avoid using pesticides from the Garden Hotline. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.recycling: Cat food cans that are clean, dry, and empty, are recyclable.
disposal_conditions.compost: Both dry and wet pet food, dog bones, and pet treats can go in the food and yard waste. They will be turned into valuable compost.

items.explanation: PET FOOD goes in the food and yard waste.  Both wet and dry pet food can be composted.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Paper pet food bags go in the recycling. Remove any string stitching first.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic pet food bags and bags made of multiple materials go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Paper pet food bags go in the recycling.  Plastic pet food bags go in the garbage. Those bags are made of multiple materials that can't be recycled.

disposal_conditions.garbage: All pet waste goes into garbage bins or pet waste stations. Bag pet waste, used cat litter, and feces. Tie off the bags and put in the garbage.

items.explanation: PET WASTE OR LITTER goes in the garbage. Bag pet waste, and used kitty litter. Tie off the bags and put in the garbage.  Pet waste cannot be composted at home or through industrial composting.  Never flush kitty litter down the toilet!

disposal_conditions.recycling: Phone books go in the recycling.

items.explanation: PHONE BOOKS goes in the recycling. You can opt out of receiving phone books.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Photographs, xray films, and slides go in the garbage. The coatings on photographs make the paper undesirable for recycling.
disposal_conditions.donate: Many craft stores will take unwanted photos or 35mm slides as donation. Seattle Recreative , a creative-reuse store relies on donations of unused/unwanted craft supplies that would otherwise end up in a landfill. There are other creative reuse stores across the city. Check out their websites and send them an email or give them a call about donations.

items.explanation: Photographs go in the garbage.   Photographs aren't recyclable because of the chemicals used to create them and the short fibers used for their paper.

disposal_conditions.compost: • Greasy or food-soiled pizza boxes go in the food and yard waste. • Remove any materials that are not paper or cardboard.

items.explanation: PIZZA BOXES go in the food and yard waste but first, remove any foil, plastic cups or pieces.

disposal_conditions.compost: Plants and trees go in the food and yard waste. Remove pots and soil. All pieces must be less than 4 inches in diameter and under 4 feet long.

items.explanation: PLANT OR TREE SALVAGE goes in food and yard waste. All pieces must be less than 4-inches in diameter and under 4-feet long.  Remove all soil.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Empty and rinsed plastic plant pots go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.compost: Plant Containers can be compostable IF the phrase "compostable" is featured. If in doubt it's better to throw the container in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Dirty or ceramic plant pots go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Clean plastic pots go in the recycling. Ceramic or dirty pots go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic berry baskets (thin green plastic mesh baskets) go in the garbage.

items.explanation: PLASTIC BERRY BASKETS go in the garbage. Thin green plastic berry baskets are not recyclable. Bring them back to the store for reuse.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Hard plastic food trays that are empty, clean, and dry and larger than 3 inches go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Thin plastic cookie trays go in the garbage, the brittle plastic has little value in recycling.

items.explanation: PLASTIC FOOD TRAYS go in the recycling. Plastic berry trays larger than 3-inches are recyclable.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Empty, clean plastic bottles • Plastic bottle caps can be recycled, if attached to the empty bottle
disposal_conditions.compost: • Bottles labeled "Compostable" or "#7 PLA"
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Bottles that contained hazardous chemicals, like pesticide bottles • Motor oil bottles • Other automotive fluid containers • Spray bottle heads

items.explanation: Empty and dry plastic bottles go in the recycling.   Put plastic caps back on plastic bottles for recycling.  Empty Bottles that had pesticides, motor oil or other hazardous fluids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic clothes hangers go in the garbage. They can't be recycled.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate plastic clothes hangers in good condition to local thrift stores .

items.explanation: Donate plastic clothes hangers in good condition to local thrift stores.   Plastic clothes hangers go in the garbage. They can't be recycled.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Empty and clean plastic cups
disposal_conditions.compost: Plastic-like containers labeled "Compostable" or "#7 PLA" go in the food and yard waste. Learn more about compostable packaging (pdf).
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Dirty plastic cups • Plastic utensils • Styrofoam cups • Plastic plates

items.explanation: Empty and clean plastic cups go in the recycle.  Plastic-like containers labeled "Compostable" or "#7 PLA" go in the food and yard waste. Dirty plastic cups, Plastic utensils and Styrofoam cups go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Plastic food containers for yogurt, margarine, dips, salsa, salad greens, etc. that are clean, dry, and empty • Plastic tubs for dishwasher and detergent pods and powders • Plastic lids 3 inches across or larger • If clean, dry, empty, and sturdy, plastic storage bins can be recycled. If broken, only recycle pieces larger than 3 inches. • If empty, clean, dry, and larger than 3 inches, plastic Tupperware items can go in your recycling bin.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Containers with food inside • Lids less than 3 inches across • If broken, pieces smaller than 3 inches should go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: If in good condition, consider giving away storage bins you no longer need to your local thrift store, or using sites like Craigslist, Freecycle, and Buy Nothing.

items.explanation: Empty, clean and dry PLASTIC TUBS larger than 3 inches go in recycling. Dirty or containers smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean, empty, and dry milk, juice, and laundry detergent jugs go in the recycling. Plastic lids and caps can be recycled if attached to the bottle.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic lids, caps and pull tabs go in to the garbage.

items.explanation: Empty milk, juice, and laundry detergent jugs go in the recycling. Plastic lids and caps can be recycled if attached to the bottle.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Lids 3 inches in diameter or larger go in the recycling. This includes: • Yogurt container lids • Margarine tub lids • Deli container lids • Cold and hot drink cup lids • Small plastic caps if screwed onto an empty plastic bottle
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Lids less than 3 inches in diameter • Loose plastic bottle caps

items.explanation: Lids larger than 3 inches go in the recycling.   Smaller or dirty lids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Rigid plastic items can go in the recycling: • Should be 3 feet or less on all sides • Small amounts of attached metal (such as hinges) are allowed • Remove other materials such as fabric, foam, or wood.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Broken plastic pieces from large rigid plastic items that are smaller than 3 inches are not recyclable. These broken pieces go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.special: Larger rigid plastic outdoor furniture that doesn't fit in your recycling cart can be set next to the cart on your recycling collection day.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate plastic patio furniture and other large plastic items in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Put large items next to your recycle cart. Rigid plastic outdoor furniture should be 3-feet or less on all sides.  Small amounts of attached metal (such as hinges) are allowed Remove other materials such as fabric, foam, or wood.

items.item_name: Plastic Bottles or Jugs, and Soda Bottles

disposal_conditions.recycling: Empty plastic bottles and jugs up to 5 gallons. Must be empty , clean , and dry . Plastic bottle caps can be recycled if attached to the empty bottle.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Bottles labeled "Compostable" or "#7 PLA"

items.explanation: This goes in the recycling, but first,  Empty them out and put the plastic bottle caps back on.

synonyms: Plastic Liners and Trays From Food Boxes

disposal_conditions.recycling: • All plastic must be clean, empty and dry to be recycled. • Yogurt containers • Deli containers • Bottles and jugs • Microwave trays • Plastic lids 3 inches or larger • Small plastic bottle caps if screwed onto an empty plastic bottle • Clean plastic plant pots • Tupperware • Pill bottles (not prescription vials) • Rigid plastic toys \- 3-feet or less on all sides • Rigid plastic sporting equipment \- 3-feet or less on all sides • Rigid plastic furniture \- 3-feet or less on all sides • Storage containers • Buckets with no fabric, foam, or wood attached
disposal_conditions.compost: Plastic-like containers labeled "Compostable" or "#7 PLA" go in the food and yard waste. Learn more about compostable packaging (pdf) .
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Plastic items less than 3 inches in diameter • Loose plastic bottle caps • Dirty plastic cups • Plastic utensils • Blister packs • Flexible plastic (such as inflatable pools) • Plastic molding • Vinyl or plastic blinds • CD and DVD cases • Plastic clothes hangers • Plastic tarps • Ziploc or plastic bread bags • Plastic liners and trays from food boxes

items.explanation: Clean rigid plastic larger than 3 inches can go in the recycling.  All other plastics go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring pool chemicals to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. Pool and spa chemicals aren't allowed in the garbage.

items.explanation: POOL OR SPA CHEMICALS are potentially hazardous. Bring pool chemicals to a household hazardous waste facility for free. Pool and spa chemicals aren't allowed in the garbage. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: Porcelain sinks, plastic parts, toilet seats, and fixtures can go in the garbage if they don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: To recycle, for a cost, drop toilets off at DTG Recycling Group . All non-porcelain parts must be removed. Bring porcelain sinks, toilets, and tubs to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: If items are in good condition for reuse, check with local salvage businesses to see if they can take them. Northwest Building Salvage Network

items.explanation: You can donate PORCELAIN SINKS, TOILETS OR TUBS in good condition. Or take them to City Transfer stations for a fee. You can often donate or resell sinks, toilets, and tubs to building supply stores that specialize in salvage materials or on sites like Craigslist and Buy Nothing groups.  Porcelain sinks and fixtures can go in the garbage if they don't exceed the weight limits printed on your bin. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Metal pots and pans can go in the recycling. Pots and pans can have a non-stick coating and small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Damaged ceramic or glass pots, pans, and lids can go in the garbage. • Any heat-treated bakeware or kitchen glass that is broken or damaged is garbage. This includes glass baking trays, microwave plates, glass bowls, and more. These types of glass are not recyclable. • Drinking glasses and glass bakeware can go in the garbage, whether they're broken or not.
disposal_conditions.donate: Cookware in good condition should be donated to your local thrift store

items.explanation: Metal pots and pans can go in the recycling, or into the garbage.  They can have small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached.   Ceramic or glass pots, pans, and lids can go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Printer cartridges can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Some printer cartridge manufactures and stores that sell printer cartridges will take old cartridges back for recycling.

items.explanation: Printer cartridges can go in the garbage.  Some printer cartridge manufactures and stores that sell printer cartridges will take old cartridges back for recycling

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Find locations to recycle printers and peripherals via the Take It Back Network (fees may apply).
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to request curbside electronics pickup. Fees apply.

items.explanation: Broken computer equipment can be picked up for a fee or dropped off at sites through E-Cycle WA to be recycled for free.  To schedule a pickup go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000

disposal_conditions.recycling: Notebooks with cardboard covers go into recycling. The covers should be removed from notebooks with a hard cover (fabric, plastic); the rest of the notebook goes into recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: If your notebook has a metal or plastic binding, remove it and put it in the garbage. These bindings are not recyclable.

items.explanation: Spiral bound notebooks go in the recycling. You don't need to remove the metal or plastic binding.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty propane or gas canisters under 2 pounds can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Tanks greater than 2 pounds and up to 30 pounds (8 gallons) must go to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee. This includes tanks that are empty or that contain any amount of propane or butane. For tanks larger than 30 pounds or 8 gallons, call our household hazards line at (206) 296-4692. Hours are 9:00 a.m. to 4:30 p.m., Monday through Friday.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a Special Item pickup for up to four under 1lb. propane canister (such as green Coleman camp gas canisters, etc.). Fees apply.

items.explanation: PROPANE TANKS are potentially hazardous. They can be dropped off at Household Hazardous facilities for free.  For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.hazardous: • Rechargeable Appliances can't go in the garbage because they contain materials harmful to people, animals, and the environment.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • King County’s Take It Back Network is a network of e-cyclers that accept a variety of electronic equipment. Use their search tool to find the closest location to you. No fee for TVs, monitors, and computers covered from households and small businesses through the E-Cycle Washington program. Fees may apply for other items. • Call2Recycle is a free take-back program for rechargeable batteries. You can find drop off locations or mail-back. No fee. They accept: • Rechargeable batteries • E-Bike batteries (participating brands) • Cellphones • Household hazardous waste: Bring batteries to a city household hazardous waste facility , where the materials will be reclaimed and recycled. No fee. • Separate batteries from devices • Maximum for a single non-auto or marine battery is 25 lbs. (lithium, other rechargeables or alkaline) • 50 pound total weight limit for any combination of non-auto batteries per visit • Up to 10 lbs. of damaged and/or recalled batteries are accepted at household hazardous waste facilities but NOT at transfer stations
disposal_conditions.special: • Submit a Special Item pickup request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 for small appliance collection. Batteries do not need to be removed before items are picked up. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate rechargeable appliances in working condition to local thrift stores or resell or give them away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing . Appliances in working condition can also be brought to the North Transfer Station and donated to via the Goodwill drop off location there.

items.explanation: Donate working condition RECHARGEABLE APPLIANCES to  thrift stores, on Craigslist, and Buy Nothing groups.   Appliances with the rechargeable batteries removed can go in the garbage.   The batteries must go to household hazardous waste facilities for free. They can't go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: The main plastic recyclables that Seattle wants collected are: • Plastic Bottles • Plastic Jugs • Plastic Containers and Tubs
disposal_conditions.compost: Containers labeled specifically #7 PLA or Compostable go in the food and yard waste. Learn more about compostable packaging (pdf).
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Styrofoam plates and cups • Plastic utensils • Dirty plates and cups with a shiny coating • Dirty plastic-like materials that aren't marked "Compostable" or "#7 PLA"

items.explanation: Empty, clean and dry plastic larger than 3 inches goes in the recycling. Dirty or small plastic items go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring up to 2 residential refrigerators and freezers per visit to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page. You must remove doors or secure doors/latches shut with duct tape or secure rope on refrigerators, freezers, and coolers.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup. You must remove doors and latches from refrigerators, freezers, and coolers. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: REFRIGERATORS OR FREEZERS can be dropped off or picked up for recycling for a fee. To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206) 684-3000.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Put sawdust or shavings in paper bags or boxes to keep from scattering. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Unpainted and untreated wood can go in the food and yard waste: • Pieces should be less than 4 inches in diameter and under 4 feet long to avoid extra fees. • Remove nails and all other non-wood materials. • Avoid extra charges, break lumber and boards down so they fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Put sawdust or shavings in paper bags or boxes to keep from scattering. • To avoid extra fees, don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Painted or treated wood can go in the garbage: • All pieces must be less than 4 inches in diameter and under 4 feet long. • Avoid extra charges: break lumber and boards down so they fit in your cart with the lid closed.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Donate or resell reusable lumber through building supply businesses that specialize in salvage or reusable materials.

items.explanation: Donate or resell reusable lumber through building supply businesses that specialize in salvage or reusable materials. Unpainted lumber can be cut up and put into the food and yard waste cart.  Or take it to City Transfer stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Reusable shopping bags cannot be recycled and go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Stained, worn out, ripped, or damaged cloth shopping bags can be turned into new products through Threadcycle . Some local thrift and second-hand stores are partners and will accept these items at their regular collection or drop box locations.

items.explanation: Reusable cloth shopping bags can go in the garbage.  Drop off stained, torn or ripped bags at locations participating in kingcounty.gov/threadcycle

disposal_conditions.garbage: Rocks and gravel can go in your garbage if they don't exceed the weight limits. It must fit in your cart with the lid completely closed. No individual rock should be larger than 2 feet by 2 feet.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring rocks and gravel to city transfer stations . They are charged as garbage. Charge for garbage is $145 per ton; minimum charge of $30 covers up to 420 lbs.
disposal_conditions.donate: Gravel in good condition may be accepted by local salvaged building material businesses.

items.explanation: ROCKS OR GRAVEL go in the garbage, or can be donated. Bring rocks and gravel to City Transfer stations for a fee. No individual rock should be larger than 2 feet by 2 feet. Gravel in good condition may be accepted by local salvaged building material businesses. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Metal parts larger than 3 inches go in the recycling. • Rusted metal can be recycled unless the metal is falling apart. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached. • Metal water bottles can go in the recycling container. • Fire place screens made of all metal can be recycled, however if it is too big to fit in your cart or is made of mixed materials, request a special item pick-up.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items with large amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber attached • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • All metal--except car parts, used duct work, and oil tanks--is accepted as recycling at city transfer stations . There is no fee for most metal. • Maximum size is 8 feet long. • There is a charge for appliances, limit 2 per load. For a list of metal recyclers, visit Salvage and Source-Separated Recyclers List on the King County website.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup. Fees apply. • Large metal parts can be put at the curb for pickup on your recycling collection day. • These must be bundled or tied. • The maximum size is 2 feet on all sides.
disposal_conditions.donate: If your fireplace screen is in good condition, consider donating it to your local thrift store, or resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist, Freecycle, and Buy Nothing.

items.explanation: Scrap metal larger than 3 inches and with minimal amounts other non-metal materials are acceptable for recycling.  Small or mixed material pieces go in the garbage.

items.item_name: Screws, Nails, Nuts, Bolts, and small metal pieces

disposal_conditions.garbage: Screws, nails, nuts, bolts, and other metal items smaller than 3 inches go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: You can drop these off at local recyclers. Please put them in a metal or other container for drop off. Check out drop off details . You can drop these off in the Scrap Metal bins at the Transfer Stations. Please put them in a metal container so they aren't loose in the bin.

items.explanation: Screws, nails, nuts, bolts, and other metal items smaller than 3-inches go in the garbage.   Small metal items can fall through the equipment at the recycling sorting facility. You can drop of small metal pieces for recycling at City Transfer Stations. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

items.item_name: Shampoo, Lotion, and Hair or Skin Care Bottles

disposal_conditions.recycling: Clean, Empty, and Dry shampoo and lotion bottles go in the recycling. Plastic lids and caps can be recycled if attached to the bottle.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Lids that are less than 3 inches in diameter that are not attached to the bottle go in the garbage. Pump tops also go in the garbage. They are made from different types of plastics and have metal springs, and they cannot be sorted for recycling. • Unwanted product (lotion, shampoo, conditioner, etc.) can be left in the bottle and thrown away.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Hair dye, hair bleach, or hair developer contain dangerous chemicals. Drop off unused or partially used product at a household hazardous waste facility.

items.explanation: Empty shampoo and lotion bottles go in the recycling. Screw plastic caps back on plastic bottles for recycling.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Sharps can be dropped off at locations througout the city. View a map of locations . Sharps can be disposed of at city transfer stations for free. View instructions for how to safely handle and dispose of sharps .

items.explanation: SHARPS - NEEDLES AND SYRINGES are a health hazard.  These can't go in the garbage; they can injure other people.  They must be dropped off at designated locations.   If you find a syringe on public property, report it as illegal dumping at (206)684-7587.  If you are a business and need to dispose of sharps, call the Households Hazards Line at (206) 296-4692.

disposal_conditions.compost: You may put small amounts of shredded paper from your home, and without any plastic or metal, in the food and yard waste cart. Keep it contained in a paper bag or mix it in with the food and yard waste, so it does not fly out and create litter. You MUST remove plastic and metal pieces from papers before shredding, as they contaminate the compost. Shredded credit cards and envelopes with plastic windows are not accepted.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: The best way to dispose of shredded paper is to drop it off at a collection site to be recycled. SPU and our partners organize recycling & reuse collection events for hard-to-recycle items throughout the year, find out dates and locations . Many private companies offer shredding services, including legal offices, realtors, and some shipping companies.

items.explanation: SHREDDED PAPER goes in the food and yard waste. If it has plastic in it, it goes in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Silverware and other metal items 3 inches or larger can go in the recycling. Small amounts of attached rubber, plastic, wood accepted.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Silverware and other metal items smaller than 3 inches on all sides can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate silverware in good condition to local thrift stores . Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Donate SILVERWARE in good condition to thrift stores, on Craigslist and Buy Nothing groups.  Or put items larger than 3 inches in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Empty butane and gas canisters under 2 pounds can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.hazardous: Small gas canisters with any amount of gas or butane can be taken to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Small "Coleman" style canisters with any amount of gas can be dropped off for free at a household hazardous waste facility . Up to 3 empty canisters per visit can be taken to a Recology Store for a fee.
disposal_conditions.special: Schedule a Special Item Pick up online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000. Fees apply. • Small camp stove and backpacking canisters should be empty of fuel • Canisters must be under 1 pound in size • Limit : 4 canisters under 1 pound in size each • Place in a small box next to your garbage cart on garbage collection day after receiving email confirmation that pick-up has been scheduled

items.explanation: Empty butane and gas canisters under 2-pounds can go in the garbage.   Empty small backpacking-style isobutane fuel canisters (excluding green "Coleman" style propane canisters) can be dropped off for free at the MSR Repair Shop, in SODO, or at the Mountaineers Bookstore, in NE Seattle.  Drop off contianers with any amount of gas or butane at household hazardous waste facilities for free. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Unusable sewing machines, toasters, blenders, food processors, microwaves, coffee makers, mixers, and other small appliances can go in the garbage. • Make sure to remove any batteries, and double check that your bin meets the weight requirements for pick up.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Some small household appliances, even with some damage, can be recycled as scrap metal for free at city transfer stations . • Bring your garbage disposal to a transfer station or to your local scrap metal collector.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup. Fee applies.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate blenders, toasters, mixers, microwaves, sewing machines, air fryers, and food processors in good condition to local thrift stores . • Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Toasters, blenders, food processors and mixers can go in the garbage.  Some small household appliances can be recycled as scrap metal for free at city transfer stations. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Photoelectric smoke detectors with batteries removed go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Ionizing smoke detectors should be sent back to the manufacturer. Find manufacturer addresses .

items.explanation: Smoke detectors with batteries removed go in the garbage.   Some smoke detector manufacturers will take back their old alarms for disposal.

disposal_conditions.compost: Cardboard with food grease or oil stains (like pizza boxes) go in the food and yard waste. Put cardboard that is mostly wet in the food and yard waste.

items.explanation: Cardboard with food grease or oil stains (like pizza boxes) and  cardboard that is mostly wet go in the food and yard waste cart.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Bring solvents like paint thinner to a household hazardous waste facility : • Put solvents in a sealed container that is 5-gallons or less. • Limit 50-gallons per trip. Solvents aren't allowed in the garbage.

items.explanation: SOLVENTS are a health hazard. Solvents aren't allowed in the garbage. Drop off  solvents like paint thinner to a household hazardous waste facility for free. Put solvents in a sealed container that is 5-gallons or less. Limit 50-gallons per trip. For Hazardous Waste facility information go to hazwastehelp.org or call at 206-296-4692

disposal_conditions.recycling: Plastic spouts don't need to be removed to recycle soup boxes. Make sure they are empty and clean.

items.explanation: SOUP BOXES go in the recycling. Cartons must be clean and empty. Plastic spouts don't need to be removed. Plastic caps go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Metal parts larger than 3 inches go in the recycling. • Small amounts of plastic, wood, or rubber can be attached.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Metal items that are smaller than 3 inches. • Metal items with plastic, wood, or rubber attached.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: All metal, except car parts, are accepted as recycling at city transfer stations . There is no fee for most metal. • Maximum size is 8 feet long. • There is a charge for appliances, limit 2 per load.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup. Fees apply. Large metal parts can be put at the curb next to your cart on your recycling collection day. • These must be bundled or tied. • The maximum size is 2 feet on all sides.

items.explanation: Stainless steel larger than 3 inches and with minimal amounts other non-metal materials are acceptable for recycling.  Small or mixed material pieces go in the garbage. Bring large amounts to City transfer stations for free. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

synonyms: Steel or Tin Cans and Lids

disposal_conditions.recycling: Recyce cans when they are clean, empty and dry. Keep can lids partially attached, push the lid into the can, or put loose lid into can and crimp shut. You don't need to remove the can label.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Loose can lids (not attached or pushed into the can) go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Empty, Clean and Dry cans go in the recycling.  Keep can lids partially attached or push the lid into the can. You don't need to remove the can label.   Loose can lids go in the garbage.

items.item_name: Steel or Tin Cans and Lids
synonyms: Steel or Tin Cans and Lids

disposal_conditions.recycling: Keep can lids partially attached or push the lid into the can. You don't need to remove the can label.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Loose can lids (not attached or pushed into the can) go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Empty, Clean and Dry cans go in the recycling.  Keep can lids partially attached or push the lid into the can. You don't need to remove the can label.   Loose can lids go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring up to 2 residential appliances per visit to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: STOVES can be dropped off or picked up for recycling for a fee. To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000 Drop off at City transfer stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.compost: Only straws that are labeled "Compostable" go into your food and yard waste.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic straws and straws that you are unable to verify that they are approved compostable go into the garbage. Plastic straws are not recyclable.

items.explanation: Plastic STRAWS go in the garbage.  Only straws that are labeled "Compostable" go into your food and yard waste.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Styrofoam, polyurethane foam blocks, packing peanuts, and foam packing sheets go in the garbage. Packing peanuts must be bagged and tied closed before placing in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Styro Recycle in Kent accepts Styrofoam blocks, packing sheets, and packing peanuts for recycling. • Seattle Public Utilities hosts six recycling drop-off events per year to collect special items like foam for recycling. The events are free, and open to all Seattle residents. Learn more here: Beyond the Cart .
disposal_conditions.special: Styrofoam blocks (expanded polystyrene) can be recyclable through Special Item Pick Up service. Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a free pickup. Polyurethane foam, packing peanuts, and foam sheets are NOT accepted through Special Item Pick Up service. Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a free pickup. Polyurethane foam, packing peanuts, and foam sheets are NOT accepted through Special Item Pick up service.

items.explanation: STYROFOAM BLOCKS can be picked up for free  Or it can go in the garbage.  To schedule a free pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000

disposal_conditions.garbage: Foam packing sheets go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Styro Recycle in Kent accepts Styrofoam blocks, packing sheets, and packing peanuts for recycling.

items.explanation: If the packing sheets are Styrofoam, you can schedule a free Special Item pickup online (www.seattle.gov/specialitems) or call SPU Customer Service (206) 684-3000.  They will be picked up for recycling. Otherwise they go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Styrofoam cups go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Styrofoam cups go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Clean, Empty, and Dry plastic containers (typically black, white, beige, or clear in color) • Clean, Empty, and Dry plastic-coated paper containers (for example, a container often used for take-out in Chinese restaurants) • Clean, Empty, and Dry paper containers - not shiny and no grease stains (for example paper food boats and trays)
disposal_conditions.compost: Plastic Stickers or labels must be removed before placing in the compost container. • Dirty or clean containers marked “Compostable” or “#7 PLA” (Not Shiny) • Greasy pizza boxes • Learn more about compostable packaging .
disposal_conditions.garbage: • All Styrofoam containers • Dirty plastic containers that are not marked "Compostable" or "#7 PLA" • Dirty plastic-coated (shiny) paper containers

items.explanation: Empty, Clean and Dry plastic containers can go in the recycling. Approved compostable containers (which indicate number seven PLA on them) go in the food and yard waste. All other and dirty plastic containers go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic tarps go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Plastic tarps go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Mercury items can't go in the garbage. Bring thermometers, thermostats, and other items containing mercury to a household hazardous waste facility . No fee.

items.explanation: THERMOMETERS OR MERCURY ITEMS are potentially hazardous. Bring thermometers, thermostats, and other items containing mercury to a household hazardous waste facility for free. Mercury items can't go in the garbage. Mercury can greatly harm your nervous, digestive, and respiratory systems. Don't handle mercury directly, and try to avoid breaking items that contain mercury.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring up to 4 tires per visit to city transfer stations . Fees apply.

items.explanation: This can be dropped off. Bring up to 4 tires per visit to city transfer stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: Tissue boxes go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Unused tissue goes in the garbage.

items.explanation: TISSUE BOXES go in the recycling.  Unused tissues go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Toys and sporting equipment made of metal that are 2 feet or less on all sides. Can include small amounts of attached plastic, rubber, or wood. • Clean rigid plastic toys and sporting equipment 3 feet or less on all sides.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Sports equipment and toys not made of mostly rigid plastic or metal, go in the garbage. • Soft plastic items like pool floaties, inflatable balls, and yoga mats go in the garbage. • Unusable sports balls go into the garbage. • Damaged helmets and other damaged saftey gear go into the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Toys and sporting equipment greater than the above size limits can be taken to city transfer stations. Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Donate toys, sporting goods, or balls in good condition to local thrift stores . • Donate helmets and safety gear in good condition. • Resell or give away toys or sporting goods in good condition using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Donate TOYS OR SPORTING GOODS in good condition to local thrift stores, on Craigslist and Buy Nothing groups. Put broken items in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.compost: Plants, trees, brush, and branches go in the food and yard waste: • Cut down to 4 inches or less in diameter and 4 feet or less in length. • Remove all soil. • Pests like aphids, scale insects, etc. ok • Avoid extra charges: Items should fit in your cart with the lid closed. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring plants, trees, and brush to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page. • All trees, brush, or branches must be 8 feet or less in length. • All pieces must be 8 inches or less in diameter.
disposal_conditions.special: Bundle trees and brush and set next to the cart. Fees apply. Check the rates page. • Tie bundles with natural twine. No plastic allowed. • Bundles must be 2 feet or less in diameter. You can put brush out in a personal plastic garbage can labeled 'Yard Waste"

items.explanation: PLANTS TREES OR BRUSH go in the yard waste. Remove all soil. Put trees and brush less than 4 feet long and 4 inches in diameter in the yard waste cart. Set out extra yard waste bundled next to your cart on collection day for a fee.  Drop off plants trees or brush at city transfer stations for a fee. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.garbage: Tyvek envelopes go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Tyvek envelopes go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Pill bottles, such as aspirin or vitamin bottles, go in the recycling.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Clear plastic prescription medicine vials go the garbage. The plastic is too brittle to recycle.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring medicines to a secure medicine return site .

items.explanation: Plastic vitamin bottles are recyclable. Put plastic lids back on plastic bottles.  These can also go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Put vitamins in a closed bottle or bag and put in the garbage.

items.explanation: Put vitamins in a closed bottle or bag and put in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring up to 2 residential appliances per visit to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: WASHERS OR DRYERS can be dropped off or picked up for recycling for a fee. To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000.  Donate or resell working washers and dryers on Craigslist and Buy Nothing groups.

disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring water heaters to city transfer stations : • Fees apply for assembled water heaters with outer jackets and insulation intact. Check the rates page. • There is no fee for water heaters with all insulation removed. To remove all insulation, the water heater must be fully dismantled and should be brought to the transfer station in pieces.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call SPU Customer Service at (206) 684-3000 to schedule a bulky item pickup.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: WATER HEATERS can be dropped off or picked up for recycling for a fee. To schedule a pick up go to seattle.gov/SpecialItems or call (206)684-3000.  Water heaters without insulation are free to drop off at City Transfer stations.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Paperboard boxes (like butter and cereal boxes) go in the recycling. They aren't true waxed cardboard. The shiny coating on these boxes is a thin layer of plastic.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Flattened waxed cardboard boxes, such as large produce boxes, go in the garbage. Modern produce and wax style boxes are plastic-coated and are not recyclable nor compostable. • Receipts/receipt paper is not recyclable and goes in the garbage.

items.explanation: WAXED CARDBOARD goes in the food and yard waste. Flatten them first.  Paperboard boxes, like butter boxes, are coated with plastic and go in the recycling.

disposal_conditions.compost: Waxed paper goes in the food and yard waste. It can't be recycled.

items.explanation: Put waxed paper in the food and yard waste. It can't be recycled.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Individual metal blind slats go in the recycling. Remove strings, cords, and plastic and put these in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic, vinyl, and wooden blinds go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .

items.explanation: Metal blinds go in the recycling.  Remove strings, cords, and plastic and put these in the garbage.   Plastic, vinyl, and wooden blinds go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Windows and window glass can go in the garbage: • Items must fit in your cart with the lid closed and be less than two feet by two feet. • Don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Bring windows to city transfer stations . Fees apply. Check the rates page.

items.explanation: WINDOWS can go to the transfer station or can go in the garbage. Bring windows to city transfer stations for a fee.  Salvage and building supply businesses may take windows in good condition. For transfer station information go to seattle.gov/transferstations

disposal_conditions.recycling: After the foil has been removed and discarded, the wine bottle itself can be recycled.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Foil from wine bottles goes in the garbage.

items.explanation: Foil from wine bottles goes in the garbage because it is too small to recycle.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Wire clothes hangers and wreath frames can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate wire clothes hangers in good condition to local thrift stores . Check with local dry cleaners. They may accept wire hangers.

items.explanation: You can donate WIRE CLOTHES HANGERS or can go in the garbage. Check with local dry cleaners. They may accept wire hangers. They do not go in the recycling. Wire hangers get tangled in the machinery at the recycling processing facility.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Wires, cables, cords, and plastic can go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: Extension and charging cords contain recyclable metals. Check King County’s What Do I Do With tool for electronics recyclers near you. Call ahead to make sure the recycler accepts your item.

items.explanation: WIRE OR CABLE goes in the garbage or it can be dropped off.   Many scrap metal dealers will buy wire and cable.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Wood clothes hangers go in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.donate: Donate wood clothes hangers in good condition to local thrift stores .

items.explanation: Donate wood clothes hangers in good condition to local thrift stores.   Wood clothes hangers can go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.compost: Clean, untreated wood shavings, scraps, and sawdust go in the food and yard waste. Put sawdust or shavings in a closed paper bag or box to contain it.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Painted or treated wood shavings, scraps, and sawdust go in the garbage. Put sawdust and shavings in a bag and tie off.

items.explanation: Clean, untreated wood shavings, scraps, and sawdust go in the food and yard waste. Put sawdust or shavings in a closed paper bag or box to contain it.   Bag painted or treated wood shavings, scraps, and sawdust to go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Ziploc or other plastic food bags go in the garbage.

items.explanation: Ziploc or other plastic food bags go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: Newer rigid 6-pack carriers, the type that completely cover the tops of the cans, are recyclable.
disposal_conditions.garbage: The flexible 6-pack carriers are garbage. This includes carriers for sports drinks, water bottles, and more rigid carriers for 25-ounces or 32-ounces metal beverage cans.
disposal_conditions.donate: Check with the brewery or restaurant to see if they will take carriers back that are in good condition. Reuse carriers if still in good condition.

items.explanation: 6-PACK PLASTIC RINGS go in the garbage. Snip the plastic rings from 6-pack cans so none of the rings are intact.

disposal_conditions.compost: Only parchment paper that comes in a box clearly labeled as "compostable" is accepted in the food and yard compost cart. This parchment paper is coated with a natural type of silicone that is accepted in commercial compost facilities. Read the packaging carefully to be sure.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Most parchment paper is coated with a chemical or a plastic layer to give it nonstick properties. This makes it not compostable. In addition, it is not possible to recycle it because the layers can't be separated. It should be put into the garbage.

items.explanation: Most parchment paper is coated with a chemical or a plastic layer to give it nonstick properties. This makes it not compostable. In addition, it is not possible to recycle it because the layers can't be separated. It should be put into the garbage.  Only parchment paper that comes in a box clearly labeled as 'compostable' is accepted in the Food & Yard compost bin. This parchment paper is coated with a natural type of silicone that is accepted in commercial compost facilities. Read the packaging carefully to be sure.

disposal_conditions.garbage: Noxious weeds (english ivy, fox tail, morning glory, knotweed, etc.) should be bagged and put in the garbage. Learn more.

items.explanation: For more information about noxious weeds, visit www.kingcounty.gov/services/environment/animals-and-plants/noxious-weeds

items.item_name: Plastic Mailers, Paper Mailers, and Paper Bubble Wrap Envelopes

disposal_conditions.recycling: Envelopes and padded envelopes that are made of only paper (no plastic) can go in your recycling bin. Cardboard envelope
disposal_conditions.garbage: At home, put plastic bag mailers/envelopes in the garbage. They are not accepted in home recycling carts. Plastic mailer Padded paper mailer/bubble wrap mailer

items.explanation: At home, put plastic bag mailers/envelopes in the garbage. They are not accepted in home recycling carts.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Cardboard boxes • Paper padded mailer For more information, visit Amazon's packaging webpage .
disposal_conditions.garbage: There are several different types of Amazon packaging. While some packaging is accepted in Seattle's recycling program, there are some types that are not accepted. • Plastic bag mailers / envelopes • Bubble pack envelope / bubble lined envelopes • Insulated pouch • Foil bubble insulation bag • Gel packs • Foil lined paper bags used for grocery deliveries

items.explanation: Put Cardboard boxes and Paper padded mailers in the recycle. All other Amazon packaging goes in the garbage, including plastic bag mailers, bubble pack envelopes, insulated pouches, foil bubble insulation bags.  Some types of plastic packaging are recyclable at drop off locations. Go to  recycling.org to find drop off locations near you.

disposal_conditions.garbage: • Plastic bags that are wet or food-soiled • Plastic produce bags • Single plastic bags • Bubble wrap
disposal_conditions.dropoff: You can return clean plastic grocery bags and other plastic films to participating grocery stores to be recycled. Use a lookup tool to find locations near you.

items.explanation: Plastic bags, wrap and film go in the garbage. They will no longer be accepted in the Recycle Cart as of January 1, 2020. This includes plastic grocery bags, bubble wrap, dry cleaner bags, newspaper bags and other similar thin plastics.

disposal_conditions.hazardous: Do not put cell phones containing batteries in garbage or curbside recycling bins. Batteries contain hazardous substances and have the potential to cause fires when improperly disposed.
disposal_conditions.dropoff: • Call2Recycle is a free take-back program for rechargeable batteries. You can find drop off locations or mail-back. No fee. They accept: • Rechargeable batteries • E-Bike batteries (participating brands) • Cellphones and cellphone batteries • High energy batteries (participating brands) • King County’s Take It Back Network is a network of e-cyclers that accept a variety of electronic equipment. Use their search tool to find the closest location to you. Fees may apply. • 1-800-RECYCLE is both a hotline and an online tool that connects you or your business to recycling services across Washington. This tool can help you find where in your community you can recycle everything from used appliances to leftover paint. Fees may apply.
disposal_conditions.special: Submit a request online or call (206) 684-3000 to schedule a pickup from your residence. The materials collected will be reclaimed and recycled. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: • Cell phones in working condition may be donated to your local thrift store . Resell or give them away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing . You can also bring working electronics to the North Transfer Station to the Goodwill drop off location. You may also consider reaching out to a local school, non-profit, or community group that may be able to use it.

items.explanation: Do not put cellphones containing batteries in your curbside trash or recycling bins. It is important to keep cell phones with batteries out of your bins because they can cause fires in trucks and transfer stations. Consider donating working cellphones to local thrift stores or charities. If not working, cell phones with batteries should be dropped off at locations identified by 1800recycle or on the website call2recycle.com. You can also submit a special item pickup online or by calling 2066843000 to have it picked up from your home. Fees apply.

items.item_name: Stickers and Labels on Food Packaging

disposal_conditions.recycling: Paper stickers and labels, when attached to recyclable materials - like envelopes or plastic containers, do not need to be removed before being placed in the recycling container.
disposal_conditions.compost: • Paper stickers on compostable food packaging are okay to leave on before placing them in the compost cart. • Plastic stickers or labels must be removed from compostable food packaging before going in the compost cart. • Plastic stickers and labels must be removed from produce before going in the compost cart. This is a major source of contamination.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic stickers and labels on food packaging labeled compostable and on produce must go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.compost: Paper tea bags can go in the compost. Remove the staples and put those in the garbage.
disposal_conditions.garbage: Plastic mesh tea bags go in the garbage.

disposal_conditions.recycling: • Clean fencing made of rigid plastic can be recycled.
disposal_conditions.garbage: • Put all soft plastic tubes and their small caps in the garbage. Some toothpaste tubes pay be labelled "recyclable" but they are not. Please put them in the garbage. • Fencing made of soft plastic, or contaminated (dirty) rigid fencing go in the garbage. • Clean plastic tubing used for irrigation goes in the garbage as it would get tangled in the recycling facilities
Broken vacuum cleaners can go in the garbage. Make sure the lid closes and you don't exceed the weight limits printed on your cart.
disposal_conditions.special: You can put a vacuum out for extra garbage pick up on collection day. Fees apply.
disposal_conditions.donate: Resell or give it away using sites like Craigslist , Freecycle , and Buy Nothing .
